items.name: Roof Recover & Overlay in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof recover and overlay for Lansing commercial buildings — what a re-cover can hide, and how to decide honestly against a tear-off.

sections.html:
What a Roof Recover Can Hide — and When It Shouldn't

Recovering an existing roof instead of tearing it off saves money and disposal cost, and for the right building it's a completely legitimate choice. The problem is that a re-cover also has a specific ability to hide a deck problem that a tear-off would expose — and the owner who chose re-cover to save money is the one who ends up paying for that mistake later, usually at the worst possible time.

Why Owners Choose Re-Cover

A recover avoids tear-off labor, disposal fees, and the risk of exposing the building interior to weather during construction — a real concern on occupied state buildings, campus dormitories, and hospital-adjacent facilities where a mid-project rain event turns from an inconvenience into a real liability. For a roof with a sound, dry deck and no more than the code-allowed number of existing layers, re-cover is a reasonable way to extend service life at a lower installed cost than a full tear-off.

We see this decision most often on occupied buildings where the owner is weighing the disruption of a tear-off against the lower cost and faster schedule of a re-cover. On a hospital-adjacent medical office building or a state agency building with public-facing hours, that scheduling difference is a real, legitimate factor in the decision, separate from the cost savings itself.

What a Re-Cover Genuinely Cannot Fix

A new membrane laid over an existing roof doesn't repair wet insulation, deteriorated deck, or compromised structural steel underneath — it just covers it. Insulation that's already lost R-value from years of moisture intrusion stays lost. A deck with corrosion at the fastener points keeps corroding, just now under two layers of roofing instead of one, harder to catch on the next inspection. This is the core risk with re-cover: it can make a genuinely bad problem invisible for years, right up until it isn't.

We've been called to state and institutional buildings where a prior re-cover was done specifically to avoid the cost of a proper deck assessment, and the deck problem that got covered over eventually required emergency tear-off — at a cost well above what a tear-off would have run the first time, on top of whatever the re-cover itself cost.

That's the scenario we try hardest to prevent. A tear-off that gets deferred through one re-cover, then discovered anyway when the deck finally fails outright, is the single most expensive path a roof can take in this market, and it's entirely avoidable with an honest moisture and deck assessment before the first re-cover decision gets made.

The Checks We Run Before Recommending Re-Cover

  • Moisture scan across the full roof field, beyond the areas with visible symptoms
  • Layer count against the applicable code limit for additional re-covers
  • Core samples at multiple locations to check deck and insulation condition directly
  • Structural load review for the added dead weight of another roofing layer
  • Drainage assessment, since a re-cover doesn't correct an underlying slope problem
  • Interior inspection for staining or structural signs that don't show from the roof surface

If any of those checks come back showing a wet deck, an already-maxed layer count, or structural concerns, we recommend tear-off instead, even though it costs more up front — because the alternative is spending re-cover money on a roof that needed the deck work all along.

We put these results in writing with core sample photos and moisture readings by roof zone, so the recommendation stands on more than our word against a lower competing bid that skipped the assessment altogether.

How This Decision Plays Out on Public and Institutional Buildings

Facilities teams working within a fixed annual capital budget sometimes feel pressure to choose the lower-cost option regardless of what the roof actually needs. That constraint is real, and there are roofs in this market where re-cover is the honest, correct answer within that budget. What we won't do is recommend re-cover on a roof that fails the moisture and deck checks just because it's the cheaper number on paper, because that decision tends to come back to the same facilities team as a much larger emergency expense a few years later.

We'd rather lose a bid to a lower number today than watch a facilities director explain an emergency tear-off to their board two years after choosing re-cover on our recommendation. That's the standard we hold the recommendation to.

Questions Owners Ask About Recover vs. Tear-Off

How can I tell if my roof is even a candidate for recover?

It comes down to deck and insulation moisture, existing layer count against code limits, and structural capacity for added weight. We check all three before recommending either option.

Is recover always cheaper than tear-off?

Usually, in the short term. But if the deck underneath has already been compromised, recover just delays and increases the eventual cost of the tear-off that was needed anyway.

How do you check what's happening under an existing roof without removing it?

Moisture scanning and targeted core samples let us assess deck and insulation condition without a full tear-off, which is how we confirm whether a re-cover is actually appropriate.

What's the code limit on how many times a roof can be recovered?

It varies by jurisdiction and existing assembly, but most codes cap the number of layers well before a building would reach it through repeated re-covers, and structural weight limits often become the binding constraint first.

We pull the applicable code section for the owner's specific jurisdiction before quoting a re-cover, rather than relying on a general rule of thumb, since Ingham, Eaton, and Clinton county jurisdictions don't all handle this identically. It's a small step that avoids a failed inspection later in the process.

If a previous contractor already recovered our roof once, can it be recovered again?

Possibly, but that's exactly the situation where a thorough moisture scan and layer count check matter most — a second re-cover on top of a first one is where problems most often go undetected.

items.name: TPO Single Ply Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: TPO roofing for Lansing government, auto-plant, and campus buildings — why mil thickness and seam welding matter more than the bid price.

sections.html:
TPO Roofing for Lansing's State and Auto-Plant Buildings

TPO has become the default single-ply membrane for a lot of the flat roof work we see across state office buildings, GM plant additions, and the distribution buildings along US-127. It's a good system when it's specified and installed correctly. It's a common source of expensive callbacks when it isn't.

Where TPO Shows Up Most in This Market

We install and repair TPO on state agency buildings around the Capitol complex, on mechanical-heavy roof sections at the Lansing Grand River and Delta Township assembly plants, and on newer retail and office construction around Okemos and Eastwood Towne Center. It's also become the go-to re-cover option over old built-up roofs on Michigan State-adjacent buildings where the university wants a reflective, code-compliant membrane without adding excessive dead load.

TPO's popularity isn't an accident. It welds cleanly, it's reflective enough to help with summer cooling load on state and campus buildings, and it handles the wide seasonal temperature swing between a January cold snap and a July heat spell better than some older membrane types.

The Bid Trap: Why the Cheapest TPO Quote Usually Costs More

Because TPO is sold by the roll and priced by the square, it's easy for a low bid to hide a thinner membrane, a wider fastening pattern, or a seam-welding crew with minimal training. On a public bid or a facilities RFP, three contractors can submit "TPO roof replacement" line items that look identical on paper and describe three very different roofs.

An owner comparing bids on price alone has no way to catch that difference until the roof is already down. We tell facilities managers to ask each bidder for mil thickness, fastening spacing, and seam-welding method in writing before comparing numbers, because those three details predict most of what fails on a TPO roof five to ten years out.

What Actually Determines How Long a TPO Roof Lasts

A handful of decisions made before the crew ever gets on the roof matter more than anything that happens afterward:

  • Membrane thickness — 60-mil versus 80-mil changes puncture and hail resistance directly
  • Fastening pattern rated for the actual wind uplift zone, not a generic minimum
  • Seam welds tested with a probe on every run, not spot-checked
  • Insulation board that's mechanically sound under the membrane, beyond simply being present
  • Flashing detail at curbs, parapets, and penetrations, where most leaks originate
  • Walkway pad placement at service paths to mechanical equipment

None of that shows up on a glossy proposal photo. It shows up in a moisture scan five years later, or it doesn't.

TPO and Michigan's Freeze-Thaw Cycle

TPO handles cold reasonably well compared to some membranes, but the freeze-thaw pattern we get in mid-Michigan is still hard on any single-ply system. Water that gets under a poorly welded seam in November doesn't just sit there — it freezes, expands the seam gap, thaws, and works further in during the next warm stretch. By the time a facilities team notices a stain on an interior ceiling tile, the water has usually been cycling through that seam for one or two winters already.

That's why we push for probe-testing every seam at installation rather than relying on a visual pass. A visual pass can miss a partial weld that looks fine in October and opens under its first hard freeze.

Rooftop Equipment Loads on Plant and Institutional Roofs

GM plant roofs and larger state buildings carry a lot of rooftop mechanical equipment — makeup air units, exhaust fans, conduit racks — and every piece of that equipment means a penetration and a curb detail. On these roofs the membrane itself is rarely the weak point; the flashing around equipment curbs is. We spend a disproportionate amount of inspection time at curb flashing on plant and institutional roofs for exactly that reason.

We've traced repeat leak calls at plant and state office roofs back to the same root cause more than once: a curb flashing detail that was fine when a mechanical unit was first set, then never resealed correctly after that unit was swapped or a new one added years later. Facilities teams should flag any rooftop equipment change to their roofer, including a routine HVAC replacement, because that's exactly the moment a flashing detail gets disturbed and doesn't always get put back the way it needs to be.

Common TPO Questions from Facilities Managers

Is TPO a good fit for a state or municipal building on a tight capital budget?

Usually yes, if the spec isn't stripped down to the bare minimum to hit a price target. The membrane cost difference between 60-mil and 80-mil is small relative to the total job; the performance difference over fifteen winters is not.

How long should a properly installed TPO roof last in this climate?

A correctly specified and installed TPO roof holds up well through multiple decades of Michigan winters, though actual service life depends heavily on foot traffic, rooftop equipment density, and maintenance follow-through.

Can TPO be installed over an existing roof?

Sometimes, if the existing deck and insulation are dry and structurally sound and code allows it. We check moisture content and layer count before recommending a re-cover instead of a tear-off.

What's the biggest mistake you see in TPO bids on public buildings?

Bids that don't specify fastening pattern or seam-welding method at all, leaving those details to be decided in the field. Those two items should be locked in before a contract is signed, not figured out on install day.

We've reviewed public bid packages for state and school buildings where three contractors submitted numbers within a few thousand dollars of each other, and only one of them had actually specified an 80-mil membrane with a tight fastening pattern rated for the building's real wind exposure. The other two were pricing a thinner system and hoping the difference wouldn't get questioned. It usually doesn't, until the roof is five years old.

Do you offer a warranty on TPO installations?

Manufacturer warranties are available depending on the system specified and are separate from our own workmanship coverage; we go over both in writing before work starts.

items.name: EPDM Commercial Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: EPDM roof repair, patching, and replacement for older Lansing-area industrial and institutional buildings — when to patch and when the seams are done.

sections.html:
EPDM Roofs on Lansing's Older Industrial and Institutional Buildings

A lot of the EPDM in this market was installed decades ago on buildings that predate most of the newer single-ply systems — older manufacturing buildings around REO Town and Grand Ledge, legacy sections of state office buildings, and mid-century retail buildings along Michigan Avenue and Saginaw Highway. Rubber roofing has a long service history here, and plenty of it is still doing its job. The question we get asked most often is whether a given roof is still one of those.

Where EPDM Still Makes Sense

For large, simple roof fields with few penetrations, EPDM is still a reasonable choice. It's forgiving of Michigan's temperature swings — it doesn't get brittle in a January cold snap the way some membranes can — and ballasted EPDM systems handle wind uplift well on buildings without a lot of rooftop clutter. We still spec it on warehouse and light-industrial buildings around Mason, Charlotte, and the I-96 corridor where the roof geometry is straightforward.

Where it struggles is on roofs with heavy equipment loads or a lot of penetrations, since every seam and flashing detail is a place for glued laps to eventually separate.

The Age Question: Patch, Re-Cover, or Replace

Most of our EPDM calls start with a facilities manager asking whether a leaking roof needs a patch or something bigger. That answer depends less on the roof's age in years and more on where the failures are clustered. A roof with isolated seam separation in one or two locations is usually a legitimate patch job. A roof with recurring leaks at multiple, unrelated seams — especially if a prior contractor has already patched those same areas once or twice — is telling you the adhesive bond across the whole field is failing, beyond the spots you can actually see.

We walk older EPDM roofs specifically looking for that pattern before quoting anything, because a patch quote on a roof that actually needs full seam replacement or a tear-off just delays the real cost and adds a line item that gets thrown away.

What We Check on an EPDM Inspection

  • Seam adhesive condition at a sample of laps across the full roof, beyond the area of the reported leak
  • Flashing at parapets, curbs, and penetrations for shrinkage or pull-away
  • Ballast displacement on loose-laid systems after wind events
  • Membrane thickness and UV degradation on unballasted sections
  • Insulation moisture content beneath suspect seam areas
  • Drain and scupper condition, since ponding accelerates seam failure

That inspection usually tells us within a day whether we're looking at a repair, a seam-replacement project, or a full re-roof.

We document all of it with photos tied to a roof plan, beyond a written description alone, because a facilities director presenting a repair budget to a board or a purchasing committee needs something more concrete than our word for it. That documentation also becomes useful later, as a baseline to compare against on the next inspection cycle.

Freeze-Thaw Cycling and Seam Failure

Glued EPDM seams move with temperature. Michigan's freeze-thaw pattern — hard cold snaps followed by warm-ups, sometimes within the same week in February and March — puts more cyclical stress on a glued seam than a climate with a steadier winter would. We track this pattern closely on roofs across Grand Ledge and Charlotte's older manufacturing buildings, where EPDM installed in the 1990s and early 2000s is now well into the age range where seam adhesive starts losing its bond strength. A seam that's marginal going into winter often shows its first real gap by spring, right around when snowmelt is sitting on the roof and has the most opportunity to get in.

This is why we recommend a fall seam check on older EPDM roofs across this market, ahead of the first hard freeze, rather than waiting for a spring leak call. Catching a marginal seam in October and re-adhering it costs a fraction of what a full interior water-damage repair costs after a winter of that same seam cycling open and shut.

Common EPDM Questions

My roof has been patched three times in the same area. What does that mean?

It usually means the patch is treating a symptom, not the cause — often a seam that's failing along its full length, well beyond the visible gap. We'd want to inspect the whole seam run before patching a fourth time.

Is ballasted EPDM a wind risk in this area?

Properly installed and maintained ballast systems perform well here, but ballast that's been displaced by prior storms and never redistributed becomes a real vulnerability in the next high-wind event. We check ballast coverage as part of any EPDM inspection.

Can you re-cover an old EPDM roof instead of tearing it off?

Sometimes, if the existing membrane and insulation are dry and the roof hasn't already been re-covered up to the code-allowed layer limit. We confirm both before recommending it.

How do you tell EPDM apart from TPO or PVC on an older roof I haven't seen documentation for?

EPDM has a distinct rubber feel and typically shows a matte black or occasionally white surface with glued rather than heat-welded seams. We confirm membrane type on site before quoting any work, since the repair materials and methods are different.

What's the average lifespan of EPDM in mid-Michigan?

Well-installed EPDM with sound seam maintenance can serve for a long time in this climate, though the seams — not the field membrane itself — are usually what determines when a roof needs attention.

items.name: PVC Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: PVC roofing for Lansing buildings with grease, chemical, or solvent exposure — dining halls, dairy processing, and plant roofs near exhaust systems.

sections.html:
PVC Roofing Where Exposure, Not Price, Should Drive the Decision

Most flat roofs in this market don't need the chemical resistance PVC offers, and we say so when asked. But a specific slice of Lansing-area buildings sit under rooftop exhaust, grease, or solvent exposure where the wrong membrane choice fails fast, and those are the roofs where PVC earns its higher price tag.

Buildings Where Exposure Actually Matters

Michigan State's dining halls and food-service buildings vent kitchen exhaust directly onto or near the roof surface, and grease-laden air degrades standard TPO and EPDM membranes over time in a way PVC resists far better. The dairy processing operation in St. Johns and similar food-and-agribusiness facilities in the region put off comparable exposure. On the industrial side, sections of the GM assembly plants with paint-shop or solvent-adjacent exhaust runs present the same problem — the membrane is being chemically attacked from above, which is a different problem than ordinary weathering.

That distinction matters because a membrane under chemical attack degrades on a schedule that has nothing to do with its rated service life.

Outside those categories, most office, retail, and light-industrial roofs in the tri-county area don't need PVC's exposure resistance and can be served just as well, and more affordably, by TPO or EPDM.

We ask about exposure history up front rather than assuming based on building type alone. A retail building that was later converted to include a restaurant tenant, for example, may have picked up kitchen exhaust exposure that wasn't part of the original roof design, and that's exactly the kind of change that should trigger a membrane review before the next re-roof.

Why Choosing the Wrong Membrane Here Fails Fast

A standard TPO membrane installed under a grease-laden kitchen exhaust stream can show visible surface degradation within a few years — well short of the service life an owner budgeted for. That's not a workmanship failure; it's a spec failure, and it's an expensive one, because by the time the degradation is visible the membrane usually needs replacement, not patching.

We ask directly about rooftop exhaust sources — kitchen hoods, paint booths, solvent vents, dust collection — before recommending a membrane on any new construction or re-roof quote, because that single question changes the right answer more than almost anything else about the building.

Skipping that question is how an owner ends up paying twice: once for a standard membrane that fails early, and again for the PVC replacement that should have gone in the first time. We'd rather have the exposure conversation before a bid goes out than after a roof is already showing degradation.

What Makes PVC Handle Exposure Better

  • Chemically resistant to greases, oils, and many industrial solvents that degrade other membranes
  • Heat-welded seams that hold up under repeated thermal cycling near exhaust sources
  • Available in thicker gauges for high-traffic mechanical areas near kitchen or plant exhaust
  • Strong performance under ponding water combined with chemical exposure
  • Reflective surface that helps offset added rooftop heat load from exhaust equipment

None of that matters much on a typical office roof. It matters a great deal on a roof sitting under active kitchen or industrial exhaust every day of the year.

We specify PVC gauge and seam detail based on the actual exposure level measured on site, not a blanket default, since a light grease exposure and a heavy solvent exposure call for meaningfully different specs even though both point toward the same base membrane type.

Installation Considerations on Campus and Plant Roofs

PVC installation on occupied campus buildings around East Lansing usually has to work around the academic calendar and dining hall operating hours, since kitchen exhaust and rooftop access can't simply be shut down mid-semester. On plant roofs, we coordinate around production schedules and often phase work by roof section so exhaust systems stay operational. Both situations require more scheduling coordination than a typical office re-roof, and we plan for that up front rather than discovering it mid-project.

We also coordinate directly with a building's mechanical contractor when the exhaust system itself needs adjustment during the roofing work, since kitchen and plant exhaust ductwork often runs through the same roof penetrations we're detailing. Getting that sequencing wrong means either the exhaust system or the new membrane ends up compromised, and neither is an acceptable outcome on an occupied building.

Timing matters just as much on plant roofs tied to production schedules. A membrane crew working near an active paint-shop exhaust run has to coordinate around production shutdown windows rather than a standard construction calendar, and that coordination usually has to be worked out weeks ahead of the actual roof date, not adjusted on the fly once crews are already mobilized.

Questions About PVC Roofing in This Market

Do I need PVC if my building doesn't have kitchen or industrial exhaust?

Probably not. For most office, retail, and standard warehouse roofs in the Lansing area, TPO or EPDM performs well at a lower material cost. We'll tell you if your building doesn't need the added expense.

Can PVC be installed over an existing membrane?

In some cases, depending on the condition of the existing roof and applicable code limits on re-cover layers. We assess the existing assembly before recommending a re-cover versus a tear-off.

Is PVC more expensive than TPO?

Generally yes, on a material basis. The added cost is worth it specifically where chemical or grease exposure would shorten the life of a standard membrane — not as a blanket upgrade.

How do you handle roofing work over an active commercial kitchen?

We coordinate scheduling around kitchen operating hours and, where needed, phase the work so exhaust systems stay functional throughout the project.

Does PVC hold up to Michigan winters as well as summer heat?

Yes — PVC performs well across the full seasonal range we see here, from hard winter freezes to summer heat near rooftop exhaust equipment.

items.name: Modified Bitumen Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Modified bitumen roof repair and replacement for older Lansing state buildings and campus facilities — torch-down risk and multi-ply redundancy explained.

sections.html:
Modified Bitumen on Older State and Campus Buildings

A lot of the flat roofs on older state office buildings and Michigan State's academic stock were built up in modified bitumen decades ago, and many of them are still there — patched, re-coated, sometimes re-covered once, but never fully replaced. Modified bitumen has a reputation for durability in this market for a reason: multi-ply systems give an owner some margin for error that a single membrane doesn't.

Why Institutional Owners Still Choose Modified Bitumen

Government and university facilities teams often hold buildings for thirty or forty years rather than sell them, and that changes the roofing calculus. A multi-ply modified bitumen system costs more up front than a single-ply membrane, but a granule-surfaced cap sheet over base and interply layers gives redundancy — a puncture through the top layer doesn't necessarily mean water reaches the deck the way it can on a thinner single-ply system. For an owner planning to hold a building for decades, that redundancy is often worth the added installed cost.

We see this decision most often on state agency buildings and MSU academic buildings where the facilities team has watched a roof age for twenty-plus years and wants a similar service life out of the replacement.

Torch-Down Risk on Occupied Buildings

Torch-applied modified bitumen is still common, but applying open flame on an occupied government building or a campus dormitory carries real fire risk that owners should weigh before signing a scope. Cold-applied and self-adhered modified bitumen systems avoid that risk entirely, at some tradeoff in installed cost and occasionally in seam performance depending on conditions during install.

We walk owners through that tradeoff directly rather than defaulting to torch-down because it's familiar. On occupied buildings with limited emergency egress options, or buildings where a fire watch adds real scheduling cost, cold-applied is often the better call even at a higher material price.

We raise this specifically on projects at MSU dormitories and state office buildings with active public hours, where an open-flame application schedule has to work around occupancy in a way that adds real coordination cost of its own. Once an owner sees both cost pictures side by side, cold-applied is frequently the more practical choice, well beyond simply the more cautious one.

What We Check Before Recommending Re-Cover vs. Tear-Off

  • Number of existing roof layers already in place, against code re-cover limits
  • Deck moisture content beneath the current membrane
  • Structural capacity for the added dead load of another modified bitumen layer
  • Condition of base flashings at parapets and equipment curbs
  • Drainage slope and whether ponding has already damaged the interply layers

Skipping that check is how an owner ends up paying for a re-cover over a deck that actually needed replacing — a mistake that costs more to unwind later than doing the tear-off correctly the first time.

We put these findings in writing, with photos and moisture readings tied to specific roof zones, so a facilities committee or a state purchasing office reviewing the recommendation can see exactly what drove the decision. That level of documentation tends to move a project through approval faster than a general narrative estimate does.

Freeze-Thaw Effects on Multi-Ply Systems

Modified bitumen handles Michigan's cold better than some other built-up alternatives, since the polymer-modified asphalt stays more flexible in low temperatures than straight asphalt does. But repeated freeze-thaw cycling still stresses flashing details and seam laps over the decades these roofs are in service, which is why base flashing condition is usually the first thing we check on an older modified bitumen roof, not the field membrane.

We've replaced base flashing alone on more than one older state building roof and resolved what the facilities team assumed was a full-roof problem, simply because the field membrane away from the parapets was still sound. That kind of targeted fix only gets found by actually walking the flashing runs, not by assuming the whole system needs replacement because one leak showed up.

Parapet flashing on older downtown Lansing buildings and some of the original state office structures often predates the current cap sheet by decades, patched piecemeal over the years rather than replaced as a system. That patchwork history is usually where we find the active leak source, even when the field membrane a few feet away is still performing fine.

Questions About Modified Bitumen Roofs

Is modified bitumen more durable than TPO for a state-owned building?

It can offer more puncture redundancy through its multi-ply construction, which matters on buildings held for decades. TPO offers other advantages, like lower installed cost and easier field repairs. The right choice depends on how long the owner plans to hold the building and what the roof's traffic and equipment load look like.

Should I be concerned about torch-down application on an occupied building?

It's worth asking your contractor directly about fire watch procedures and whether a cold-applied or self-adhered alternative makes sense for your building. We raise this proactively on any occupied structure.

How many layers of modified bitumen can be re-covered before a tear-off is required?

Code limits vary and we confirm the applicable limit for your building before recommending a re-cover, but most jurisdictions cap re-covers well before an unlimited number of layers, and structural weight becomes a factor before that limit is even reached.

Can modified bitumen be repaired in winter?

Emergency repairs can be made in cold weather with appropriate cold-weather materials and technique, though full re-roofing work is generally scheduled for warmer months when membrane and adhesive performance is more predictable.

items.name: Built-Up Roofing (BUR) in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Built-up gravel and tar roof repair for legacy Lansing downtown and campus buildings — when maintenance still makes sense and when it doesn't.

sections.html:
Legacy Gravel Roofs Still in Service Downtown and on Campus

Walk the older sections of downtown Lansing or the original academic core at Michigan State and you'll still find built-up roofs under the gravel — layers of asphalt and felt that were standard practice for decades before single-ply membranes took over. Some of these roofs are forty, fifty, even sixty years old. A surprising number of them are still functionally sound, which is exactly why the maintain-versus-replace decision on a BUR roof deserves more thought than a reflexive tear-off.

Why Some of These Roofs Are Still Working

Built-up roofing's redundancy comes from sheer mass — multiple plies of felt saturated with asphalt, capped with gravel ballast that shields the membrane from UV and mechanical damage. A well-maintained BUR roof with intact flashings and functioning drains can outlast a lot of newer single-ply installations, simply because there's more material standing between the weather and the deck. We inspect these roofs on their actual condition, not their age on paper, before recommending anything.

That said, a lot of downtown Lansing's older commercial buildings and some of the state's legacy office stock are approaching or past the point where continued patching costs more than it saves.

Reading the Real Condition Under the Gravel

Gravel ballast hides a lot. A roof can look intact from a casual walk while the felt underneath has already delaminated in multiple spots. We pull gravel back at several sample locations, check for blistering and ply separation, and probe suspect areas rather than relying on a surface-level walk, because the gravel is specifically designed to protect — and conceal — what's underneath it.

On a typical inspection we'll pull gravel back at a dozen or more locations spread across the roof field, well beyond the area of the reported leak, since a BUR roof's failure pattern rarely stays confined to one spot once ply separation has started.

What We Evaluate on a Legacy BUR Roof

  • Ply separation and blistering at sample cuts across the field
  • Flashing condition at parapets, which fail before the field membrane usually does
  • Drain bowl condition and whether ponding has become chronic
  • Structural load capacity, since gravel ballast adds real weight over a large roof
  • Interior ceiling and structural steel for signs of long-term moisture intrusion
  • Whether prior patch work addressed the cause of a leak or just its symptom

Weight matters more on BUR roofs than on almost any other system we work on. A roof that's been re-covered once or twice already, with gravel ballast on top of it all, can be carrying more dead load than the original structural design accounted for — worth confirming with a structural review before adding anything else.

We've seen this specifically on older downtown Lansing commercial buildings where a second BUR layer was added decades ago without anyone revisiting the structural math. The roof performed fine for years, right up until a heavy wet snow load exposed how little margin was actually left.

A structural review before adding any layer is a modest cost next to what a roof collapse or a partial deck failure would mean for a downtown commercial building or a state office structure still in active use. We treat that review as a standard step on any BUR project involving a building more than a few decades old, not an optional add.

The same logic applies to buildings on Michigan State's older academic campus, where several structures still carry original built-up roofs from well before current load codes were adopted. A structural review there isn't about distrust of the original design — it's about confirming decades of accumulated repairs haven't quietly pushed the roof past what that design assumed.

The Owner's Cost Calculation

For a building an owner plans to hold for the long term — which describes a lot of downtown Lansing and state-held property — the real question isn't whether BUR is an old technology. It's whether the specific roof in front of us still has functional redundancy left, or whether continued patching is just delaying a tear-off that's already necessary. We try to answer that plainly rather than defaulting to either "replace everything" or "it'll be fine," because both defaults have cost an owner money in this market.

For state-held buildings specifically, we try to time any recommended tear-off to align with the facilities team's budget cycle where possible, rather than forcing an emergency decision. A roof that's degrading slowly, without active interior leaks, usually gives an owner enough runway to plan the replacement properly instead of scrambling.

Common Questions About Built-Up Roofs

Is a fifty-year-old built-up roof automatically a replacement candidate?

No. Age alone doesn't determine condition. We've inspected BUR roofs from the 1970s that were still performing, and BUR roofs half that age that had already failed structurally underneath. Condition drives the decision, not the install date.

Why does gravel ballast make inspection harder?

Because it sits on top of the membrane by design, protecting it from UV and impact — but that same layer also hides blistering, ply separation, and early leak points from a casual visual inspection.

Can a built-up roof be re-covered with a single-ply membrane instead of more BUR?

Often yes, if the deck is dry and structurally sound and the layer count is within code limits. This is one of the more common upgrade paths we recommend on legacy downtown buildings.

Does the weight of gravel ballast ever become a structural problem?

It can, particularly on roofs that have been re-covered more than once. We recommend a structural review before adding any additional layer to a gravel-ballasted BUR roof.

items.name: Standing Seam Metal Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Standing seam metal roofing for Lansing-area distribution and industrial buildings — snow-shedding performance and clip specification for heavy wet snow.

sections.html:
Standing Seam Metal for Distribution and Plant Buildings

Standing seam metal has become the default choice for a lot of new industrial and distribution construction along the I-96, I-69, and US-127 corridors around Lansing, and for good reason — it sheds Michigan's heavy wet snow better than almost any low-slope alternative, if the clip system and panel gauge were actually specified for our snow load, not a generic regional minimum.

Where Standing Seam Fits Best in This Market

New distribution buildings near the I-96/I-69 interchange, expanded warehouse space around Delta Township and DeWitt Township, and plant additions tied to the auto supply chain increasingly go up with standing seam roofs rather than flat single-ply. Owners like the reduced long-term maintenance and the clean water-shedding profile, especially on buildings with steeper roof pitches where standing water was never going to be the concern that snow load and wind uplift are.

It's less common on older downtown and campus buildings, most of which were built flat and would require a structural retrofit to accept a pitched metal system — that's a bigger project than most re-roofs and usually only makes sense during a larger building renovation.

We've fielded this question most often from owners of older flat-roofed buildings near Delta Township and the US-127 corridor who saw a neighboring plant addition go up in standing seam and wondered if their own building should follow. In most of those cases, a well-specified single-ply system on the existing flat roof made more financial sense than a structural retrofit to accept a pitched metal system.

Why Clip Specification Is the Decision That Actually Matters

Standing seam performance comes down to the concealed clip system more than almost any other component. A clip system underspecified for our snow and wind load can allow panels to walk under thermal expansion and contraction — Michigan's temperature swing between a January cold snap and a summer heat wave is significant — leading to seam separation years before the panel material itself would have failed. We've inspected relatively new standing seam roofs with premature seam problems traced directly back to a clip system chosen to hit a price point rather than the actual load requirements for the building.

Panel gauge matters too, but it's usually the more visible spec on a bid. Clip spacing and float allowance are the ones that get quietly value-engineered out, and they're the ones that determine whether the roof performs for thirty years or needs seam repairs in year eight.

We ask every standing seam bidder for their clip spacing and float allowance in writing before comparing prices, the same way we push on TPO fastening patterns. It's a small ask that catches most of the corner-cutting before a contract is signed rather than after a panel starts walking.

Snow-Shedding and Roof Design

Mid-Michigan gets heavy, wet snow more often than the light powder some other regions see, and wet snow is heavier per inch and more prone to sliding in sudden sheets once a roof surface warms slightly. On buildings near loading docks, walkways, or parking, snow guard placement matters as much as the roof system itself — a standing seam roof without snow retention over an entrance or dock area can turn a normal winter storm into a liability problem the first time a slab of wet snow releases at once.

We see this most on newer distribution buildings along I-96 and I-69, where loading dock traffic runs through the winter regardless of conditions. A snow guard system at the eave over those dock doors is a modest add during construction and a genuine headache to retrofit correctly after the fact.

What We Specify Before Recommending a System

  • Clip type and spacing rated for the building's actual wind uplift and snow load zone
  • Panel gauge appropriate to span and expected foot traffic for maintenance access
  • Underlayment suited to condensation risk on unconditioned warehouse space
  • Snow retention system placement at entrances, docks, and walkways
  • Fastener-free field seam detail to eliminate the exposed-fastener failure points common on cheaper systems
  • Expansion joint placement on longer roof runs to manage thermal movement

Most of those items are invisible on a finished roof and easy to skip on a bid that's competing on price. We spell them out in writing before a contract is signed so the owner knows exactly what they're paying for.

Owners evaluating competing bids on a new distribution building should ask each contractor to walk through this same list line by line rather than comparing a single lump-sum number, since two bids that look close on price can represent very different roofs once these details are accounted for.

Questions About Standing Seam in This Climate

Is standing seam worth the added cost over a flat single-ply system for a new warehouse?

For a pitched roof, generally yes — the reduced long-term maintenance and superior snow-shedding usually justify the higher installed cost over the building's life, though the comparison only makes sense on buildings designed for a pitched roof to begin with.

Do I need snow guards on a standing seam roof here?

If there's a walkway, entrance, loading dock, or parking area beneath any part of the roof, yes. Wet Michigan snow can release in large sheets, and snow retention is a straightforward way to manage that risk.

How does temperature swing affect standing seam performance?

Panels expand and contract significantly between winter and summer here, which is why clip systems need enough float allowance to accommodate that movement without stressing the seams. This is a spec detail, not something fixed after installation.

Can standing seam be installed on an existing flat roof building?

Only with a structural retrofit to create the necessary pitch and support, which is a substantially bigger project than a typical re-roof. We evaluate this on a case-by-case basis tied to a larger renovation.

items.name: Metal R-Panel Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: R-panel metal roofing for Lansing-area warehouses and light manufacturing — the maintenance tradeoff owners should know before choosing exposed fastener.

sections.html:
R-Panel Metal for Warehouses and Light Manufacturing

R-panel is the exposed-fastener metal system we see most often on straightforward warehouse and light-manufacturing buildings around Mason, Charlotte, Grand Ledge, and the smaller industrial pockets along I-96 and I-69. It's a legitimate, cost-effective choice for the right building. The tradeoff owners should understand going in is maintenance, not installation quality.

Why R-Panel Gets Chosen

R-panel costs meaningfully less to install than standing seam, largely because the panels fasten directly through the surface into the structure rather than relying on a concealed clip system. For a simple warehouse or ag-adjacent industrial building with a straightforward roof plane and a modest budget, that installed cost difference is real money, and R-panel performs perfectly well for buildings in that category.

We spec it regularly on light-industrial buildings around Mason and Charlotte, and on some of the smaller distribution and storage buildings out toward Webberville and Fowlerville along I-96, where the roof is functional rather than architectural and the owner's priority is keeping the building dry at a reasonable price.

We also see it on ag-adjacent buildings around Perry, Webberville, and Stockbridge, where the roof covers equipment storage or light processing space rather than finished office area, and the owner's main concern is straightforward weather protection rather than appearance or long-term hands-off maintenance.

The Fastener Maintenance Tradeoff Owners Need to Know

Every exposed fastener on an R-panel roof is a potential leak point, and every one of them needs periodic attention. The neoprene washers under each fastener degrade with UV exposure and temperature cycling over time, and once they harden and crack, water finds its way in at that specific point rather than across a broad membrane failure. This isn't a defect — it's how the system is designed — but it does mean R-panel roofs need a maintenance rhythm that standing seam roofs largely avoid.

We tell owners this directly before they choose R-panel over standing seam: the lower installed cost comes with an ongoing maintenance obligation that has to be budgeted for, not skipped, if the roof is going to perform for its full expected life.

We put this in writing as part of the proposal itself, with a suggested inspection interval, rather than leaving it as a verbal aside during the sales conversation. An owner who signs off on R-panel should know exactly what the maintenance commitment looks like before the roof goes up, not five years later when the first fastener starts backing out.

What Freeze-Thaw Does to Exposed Fasteners

Michigan's freeze-thaw cycling is particularly hard on exposed fastener systems. As the metal panel and the fastener expand and contract at different rates through repeated temperature swings, the fastener hole can enlarge slightly over years, and a washer that's already stiffened with age loses its ability to seal that slightly larger hole. That's the mechanism behind most of the R-panel leak calls we get in late winter and early spring, right as the freeze-thaw cycle has been running hardest.

We see this pattern reliably on older R-panel buildings around Grand Ledge and the smaller industrial parks along I-69, where roofs installed fifteen to twenty years ago are now at the point where washer degradation shows up across the whole field at once rather than at isolated spots. A single maintenance visit at that stage can address most of it before individual leaks start appearing one at a time.

Owners who skip that window usually end up calling us for leak repair anyway, just spread across several separate visits instead of one planned maintenance stop. The total labor cost ends up higher, and the roof spends longer with active leaks doing damage before anyone catches the pattern.

What a Proper R-Panel Maintenance Visit Covers

  • Fastener and washer inspection across the full roof field, beyond the areas already showing problems
  • Re-torquing or replacing fasteners that have backed out or show washer degradation
  • Sealant condition at ridge caps, eave trim, and penetration flashing
  • Panel lap condition at side and end seams
  • Rust or coating breakdown at cut edges and fastener heads
  • Gutter and downspout condition, since R-panel sheds water fast and needs functioning drainage

A five-year fastener maintenance check on an R-panel roof is inexpensive compared to what it prevents. Skipping that check is the single most common reason we see R-panel roofs fail earlier than the panel material itself would justify.

We schedule these checks proactively for owners who ask us to, rather than waiting for a leak call, since a planned maintenance visit costs a fraction of an emergency repair call and lets us catch fastener wear before it becomes an interior water problem.

Questions About R-Panel Roofing

Is R-panel a bad choice compared to standing seam?

Not at all, for the right building. It's a lower-cost system with a real ongoing maintenance obligation. Standing seam trades a higher installed cost for lower long-term maintenance. Both are legitimate choices depending on the owner's budget and how the building is used.

How often do fasteners need to be checked on an R-panel roof?

We generally recommend an inspection every few years, more often on older roofs or ones that have already shown fastener-related leaks, given how hard the freeze-thaw cycle here is on exposed fastener systems.

Can a leaking R-panel roof be repaired without full replacement?

Usually yes. Most R-panel leaks trace back to specific fastener or flashing failures rather than a failure of the panel material itself, which means targeted repair is often the right first step rather than a full tear-off.

Does R-panel handle Michigan snow load as well as standing seam?

Both systems shed snow effectively when specified correctly for the building's load requirements. The bigger practical difference between the two is long-term maintenance, not snow performance.

items.name: Silicone Roof Coatings in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Silicone roof coatings for aging Lansing-area institutional and government roofs — a capital-budget alternative to replacement, and its real limits.

sections.html:
Silicone Coatings as a Capital-Budget Alternative to Replacement

When a state agency or a school district facilities team tells us their roof capital budget won't cover a full replacement this cycle, silicone coating is often the honest answer — not because it's cheap, but because it's one of the few restoration options that actually stands up to standing water, and standing water is the specific problem on a lot of older flat roofs in this market.

Where a Coating Genuinely Solves the Problem

Aging EPDM, modified bitumen, and built-up roofs across state office buildings and older school and institutional stock frequently develop chronic ponding as drains settle or roof slope shifts slightly over decades. Silicone coating is one of the only restoration products that holds up under standing water rather than degrading from it, which makes it a legitimate option — not a stopgap — for a roof with ponding that's otherwise structurally sound.

We recommend it most often on roofs that have years of useful service life left in the underlying membrane or deck, where the problem is surface degradation and ponding-related wear rather than structural failure underneath.

This comes up often on Michigan State facilities and older school district buildings in the surrounding townships, where a roof replaced two or three decades ago is showing surface wear and chronic ponding but the underlying structure has held up. Coating that roof buys the facilities team real years without committing the current budget cycle to a full replacement.

We put a realistic timeline on that extension when we propose it, rather than implying the coating resets the roof's clock to zero. An honest estimate of the added years lets a facilities director plan the eventual full replacement into a future capital cycle instead of being caught off guard by it.

How We Decide Coat vs. Replace

This is the decision where owners get oversold most often in this industry, in both directions — some contractors push coatings on roofs that are genuinely past saving, and others push full replacement on roofs that had years of life left in them. We base the recommendation on what the moisture scan and core samples actually show, not on which service is more profitable to sell.

  • Deck and insulation moisture content — a coating over wet insulation just traps the problem
  • Existing membrane elasticity and seam condition beneath the surface
  • Extent and chronicity of ponding, and whether drainage can realistically be corrected first
  • Remaining structural life of the deck itself
  • How many capital budget cycles the owner needs the roof to last

A coating applied over a roof with wet insulation underneath doesn't fix anything — it just delays the visible symptom while the underlying problem keeps getting worse. We won't recommend coating a roof that fails that moisture check, even if it's the answer the budget wants to hear.

We put the moisture scan results in writing, roof zone by roof zone, so a facilities director can see exactly where a coating is appropriate and where it isn't. On a large state or campus roof, the answer is sometimes both — a coating over the sound majority of the field, paired with targeted repair or partial tear-off over a wet section.

Silicone's Ponding Water Advantage

Most restoration coatings, including acrylic, break down under sustained standing water. Silicone doesn't have that weakness, which is exactly why it's the right choice for the ponding-prone flat roofs common on older state and institutional buildings where drainage was never quite right to begin with and correcting the slope isn't in the current budget.

We've coated roofs across state office buildings and older school stock in this market that had ponding for as long as anyone on the facilities team could remember. Correcting the slope outright would mean tapered insulation and a much larger project; silicone coating let the owner protect the roof now and revisit the drainage question on a future capital cycle.

What a Silicone Coating Project Actually Involves

A proper silicone coating job involves more than spraying material over an old roof. It starts with pressure washing and surface prep, addressing any active leaks and deteriorated flashing before coating goes down, and applying at a manufacturer-specified thickness that actually delivers the warranted performance. Skipping surface prep to save a day of labor is the most common reason a silicone coating underperforms its expected service life.

We photograph and document surface prep and flashing repair work before coating goes down, specifically for owners who need that record for a facilities file or a public procurement audit trail. It's a small step that protects the owner if a warranty question ever comes up years later.

Questions About Silicone Coatings

Will a silicone coating fix a roof that already has interior leaks?

Only if we address the underlying leak sources and flashing issues before coating — the coating restores and protects the field membrane, it doesn't fix an active penetration or flashing failure on its own.

How long does a silicone-coated roof typically last before recoating?

It depends on the coating thickness applied, existing membrane condition, and traffic on the roof, but a properly specified system can extend a roof's service life by a meaningful number of years before a follow-up coat or replacement is needed.

Is silicone better than acrylic for our roof?

If your roof has ponding water, yes — acrylic isn't rated for sustained standing water and silicone is. If ponding isn't an issue, acrylic can be a more cost-effective option worth considering.

Can you tell if a roof is a good coating candidate before doing a full inspection?

Only roughly. A real answer requires moisture scanning and, usually, core samples to confirm the deck and insulation are dry enough for a coating to make sense rather than just delaying an eventual tear-off.

items.name: Acrylic Roof Coatings in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Acrylic roof coatings for Lansing office and retail roofs without ponding — a lower-cost restoration option, and where it's the wrong choice.

sections.html:
Acrylic Coatings: The Right Tool When Ponding Isn't the Problem

Acrylic coating gets recommended too often on roofs where it's the wrong material, usually because it's cheaper than silicone and a contractor is pricing to win the bid rather than fit the roof. Used correctly, on the right roof, it's a genuinely cost-effective way to extend service life and cut cooling load. Used on a roof with standing water, it fails early and an owner ends up paying twice.

Where Acrylic Is a Sound Choice

Office buildings, retail strips along Cedar Street and Saginaw Highway, and well-drained warehouse roofs without ponding issues are good acrylic candidates. On roofs with adequate slope and functioning drainage, acrylic's high reflectivity does real work reducing summer cooling load, which matters on buildings with rooftop HVAC units already working hard through mid-Michigan's humid summer stretch. It's also considerably less expensive per square than silicone, which makes it attractive for owners managing multiple properties on a set restoration budget.

We see this most often with retail chain operators and property management firms holding several buildings across the Lansing area, where a per-square cost difference across multiple roofs adds up fast. For a portfolio without chronic ponding issues, acrylic can meaningfully stretch a restoration budget further than committing every roof to silicone.

Where Acrylic Is the Wrong Choice — and Why It Still Gets Sold

Acrylic coatings are water-based and simply aren't built to withstand sustained standing water. On a roof with chronic ponding — common enough on older flat roofs across this market where drains have settled slightly over the decades — an acrylic coating will soften, degrade, and fail well ahead of its expected service life. We've inspected acrylic-coated roofs less than five years old with visible coating breakdown concentrated exactly where water was standing, on roofs where silicone would have been the correct spec from the start.

The reason this keeps happening is straightforward: acrylic costs less per square, so it wins price-driven bids even on roofs where it's the wrong material. An owner comparing two coating bids without knowing this distinction has no way to catch the problem until the coating is already failing.

We walk every coating candidate roof specifically looking for ponding before quoting acrylic, and we'll say so directly if a roof isn't a fit, even though silicone costs more per square. Losing that portion of the bid is a better outcome for us than watching a coating fail in eighteen months and having our name attached to it.

What We Check Before Recommending Acrylic Over Silicone

  • Drainage survey confirming no chronic ponding areas across the full roof
  • Roof slope and drain spacing relative to typical mid-Michigan storm volume
  • Existing membrane condition and remaining flexibility
  • Rooftop equipment and foot traffic patterns that could wear a thinner coating faster
  • Owner's priority — cooling load reduction versus maximum weather resistance

If that drainage survey turns up ponding, we recommend silicone instead, even though it costs more, because an acrylic coating on a ponding roof is money spent on a system that won't hold up.

We run this same survey even on roofs where the owner has already decided they want acrylic for the lower price, because that decision only makes sense once we've confirmed drainage actually supports it. An owner who insists on acrylic over a ponding roof after being told the risk is one we'll still work with, but only after that conversation happens in writing.

Reflectivity and Summer Cooling Load

Mid-Michigan summers bring enough sustained heat and humidity that rooftop HVAC load matters on office and retail buildings, particularly ones with flat black membrane absorbing heat all day. A reflective acrylic coating on a well-drained roof can measurably ease that load on the mechanical system, which is a real, tangible benefit for an owner beyond just weatherproofing.

We factor this into recommendations for retail and office buildings along Saginaw Highway and around Frandor, where older rooftop units are already working at close to capacity through Michigan's humid late-summer stretch. A reflective coating won't replace an aging HVAC system, but it measurably reduces how hard that system has to run on the hottest days.

We check the age and condition of the rooftop units themselves before pitching the energy angle too hard, since a coating's cooling benefit matters most to an owner who's trying to stretch the life of existing equipment rather than one who's already planning an HVAC replacement. Framing the benefit accurately keeps the recommendation useful instead of oversold, and we also factor building orientation into it — a roof with a large unshaded southern exposure gets more practical benefit from a reflective coating than one tucked between taller neighboring buildings.

Common Questions About Acrylic Coatings

How do I know if my roof has ponding water that would rule out acrylic?

The clearest way is a roof walk 48 hours after a rain event — if water is still standing anywhere on the field, that's a ponding area, and it's worth having assessed before choosing a coating.

Is acrylic coating a permanent fix?

No coating is permanent. Acrylic extends the service life of a sound underlying roof and typically needs recoating on a maintenance cycle to keep performing.

Can acrylic coating help lower my energy bills?

On a building with significant rooftop HVAC load and a dark existing membrane, a reflective acrylic coating can meaningfully reduce cooling demand during Michigan's warmer months, though the exact impact depends on the building and system.

What happens if acrylic is applied to a roof with ponding anyway?

The coating typically softens and breaks down at the ponding location well ahead of schedule, requiring premature recoating or repair in exactly the areas that needed the most protection.

items.name: Roof Drains and Scuppers in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Clearing and correcting roof drainage on Lansing commercial buildings, where freeze-thaw cycling turns a blocked drain or scupper into an ice dam and interior leak.

sections.html:
Keeping Drains and Scuppers Working Through a Lansing Winter

A blocked drain does more than cause ponding. It turns a normal Michigan freeze-thaw cycle into an ice dam that backs water up under the membrane. Most of the drainage problems we get called about started weeks earlier as a routine clog that nobody cleared before the first freeze locked it in place.

Why Drainage Failure Hits Harder in a Freeze-Thaw Climate

In a warmer climate, a partially blocked drain mostly means slow-draining ponding water and the algae or debris problems that come with standing water. In mid-Michigan, that same partially blocked drain freezes solid during a cold snap, and the next thaw sends meltwater across the roof with nowhere to go, finding every marginal seam and flashing detail on its way to somewhere it can escape. That is why we treat drain clearing as the highest-priority item on any pre-winter maintenance visit, beyond a routine housekeeping task.

Scuppers, which drain through the parapet wall rather than down through the roof deck, have their own freeze risk: ice can form right at the scupper opening and seal it shut, even when the drain line behind it is clear.

Ponding Water and What It Actually Costs a Roof

Standing water that takes longer than 48 hours to drain after a rain event accelerates membrane aging in that area far faster than the rest of the roof, and it adds real structural load that most low-slope roofs were not designed to carry indefinitely. On flat institutional and plant roofs across the Lansing area, we regularly find low points that have been ponding for years without anyone tracking it as a problem, because it never quite produced a visible interior leak — until it does, usually during a wet snow season when the extra weight from meltwater sitting on top of already-ponded water pushes a marginal deck section past its limit.

Diagnosing a Drainage Problem

We start by mapping where water actually sits after a rain event, rather than trusting the original roof plan's slope design, since roofs settle and shift over the years in ways drawings do not capture. Low points get flagged, drain and scupper capacity gets checked against the actual roof area they serve, and we look for undersized or clogged drain lines that were adequate when the building was new but are now handling a roof area that has grown through additions or rooftop equipment changes.

What a Drainage Correction Involves

  • Clearing existing drains, scuppers, and gutter lines of accumulated debris
  • Tapered insulation crickets built to redirect ponding toward existing drain points
  • Additional drain or scupper capacity where the existing count is undersized for the roof area
  • Drain bowl and strainer replacement where corrosion or damage is restricting flow
  • Heat cable installation at scuppers or drain lines in locations with a repeated freeze-shut history
  • Documentation of ponding locations and duration to support future capital planning

Not every ponding issue requires a major tapered insulation project. Sometimes clearing and upsizing a single undersized drain solves a low point that looked like it needed a full re-slope.

Scupper-Specific Problems on Parapet Buildings

Buildings with parapet walls around the roof perimeter — common on downtown Lansing commercial structures — drain primarily or partially through scuppers rather than interior drains. Scupper liners corrode over time, and the through-wall opening itself can develop gaps that let water into the wall assembly rather than out of it. We check scupper liner condition as its own item, separate from general drain clearing, because it is a different failure mode with different consequences.

Drain Capacity as a Roof Ages

A drainage system that was adequate when a building was constructed does not necessarily stay adequate as roofs get recovered, rooftop equipment gets added, or additions change how much roof area feeds into the same drain lines. We calculate whether existing drain and scupper capacity still matches current roof area whenever we are scoping a reroof or major repair, since undersized drainage on a newly recovered roof just repeats the same ponding problem the old roof had, dressed up in new membrane. That check is inexpensive compared to correcting a drainage shortfall after the new membrane is already installed and warrantied.

Drainage Questions From Owners

How often do roof drains need to be cleared?

At minimum during pre-winter and spring maintenance visits, with additional clearing after major storms that bring debris onto the roof, like heavy leaf drop or storm wind carrying material from nearby trees.

What does it mean if water is still standing 48 hours after a storm?

That is generally considered excessive ponding and points to either a drainage capacity problem or a low point that needs correction, not something to leave unaddressed.

Can heat cable actually prevent scuppers from freezing shut?

Yes, in locations with a documented history of repeated freeze-shut problems, heat cable at the scupper opening and drain line is an effective fix that avoids repeating the same emergency every winter.

Do we need more drains than our roof currently has?

Sometimes. If the roof area has grown through additions or the original drain count was undersized to begin with, adding capacity is often cheaper than repeatedly repairing ponding-related membrane damage.

Is ponding water always a sign of a structural problem?

Not always, but persistent ponding does add load the roof structure has to carry, and we recommend a structural review if ponding has been present for an extended period without correction.

items.name: Edge Metal, Coping, and Gutters in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Wind-uplift fastening, parapet coping joints, and gutter repair for Lansing commercial roofs, where freeze-thaw cycling and storm wind cause the most damage.

sections.html:
Edge Metal and Coping Are Where Most Lansing Roof Failures Actually Start

Owners tend to focus on the membrane in the field of the roof, but the edges are where wind gets its first grip and where freeze-thaw movement does the most repeated damage. A parapet coping joint that has opened a half-inch, or edge metal fastened with a pattern that predates current wind-uplift standards, is often the real cause of a leak an owner is chasing somewhere else on the roof.

Why Edges Fail Before the Field of the Roof Does

Wind does not push evenly across a roof, it concentrates uplift force at corners and edges, which is exactly why building codes specify tighter fastening patterns in those zones than in the field. Older Lansing commercial buildings — plenty of them near downtown, around REO Town, and around the original GM plant corridors — were built to wind standards that have since been revised upward. Edge metal fastened to an outdated pattern is the first thing to move in a strong spring thunderstorm or a derecho-level wind event, and once it starts working loose, the membrane behind it becomes vulnerable too.

Freeze-thaw cycling adds a second stress that has nothing to do with wind. Coping joints and edge metal seams expand and contract through repeated freezing and thawing all winter, and a joint that was sealed tight in October can be visibly open by March if the sealant or mechanical connection was not built to handle that movement.

Coping Joint Failures on Downtown and Institutional Buildings

Parapet walls on older downtown buildings and civic structures near the Capitol commonly use metal coping caps that rely on lap joints and sealant to stay watertight. Once that sealant fails from age and thermal cycling, water gets behind the coping and runs down inside the wall assembly, which can produce interior damage well below the roof line, beyond the roof deck itself. We check coping condition on every inspection specifically because this failure mode is easy to miss from the roof surface alone.

Gutters and Downspouts on Sloped Sections

Not every commercial building in the Lansing area is flat-roofed. Standing seam and metal panel sections on some retail and light-industrial buildings drain through gutters and downspouts, which have their own seasonal failure points: ice buildup that pulls a gutter section loose from its hangers, and debris blockage that causes overflow right at the fascia line where water damage is expensive to repair. We size and space gutter hangers for the actual snow and ice load the building sees, not a generic residential spec, and we check hanger spacing against the roof's actual drainage volume rather than assuming the original installation accounted for it correctly.

What an Edge and Coping Repair Scope Covers

  • Fastening pattern review against current wind-uplift requirements for the building's height and exposure
  • Coping joint resealing or replacement where lap joints have opened
  • Gutter and downspout hanger inspection, with reinforcement where ice loading has stressed the attachment
  • Termination bar and membrane edge securement check where the membrane meets the metal
  • Corner and transition detailing, since these are the highest-stress points on any roof edge
  • Documentation of any prior repair work that was not integrated properly into the original system

A full edge metal replacement is not always necessary. Sometimes reinforcing the fastening pattern and resealing coping joints extends the life of the existing system by years at a fraction of full replacement cost, and we say so when that is the honest recommendation.

What Happens When Edge Failure Goes Unaddressed

Edge metal and coping problems rarely stay small. A single loose section of edge metal in a storm can peel back a run of membrane behind it, turning what would have been a minor resealing job into a membrane repair or replacement conversation. We have seen a single open coping joint on a downtown parapet wall lead to interior water damage two floors below the roof, well after the point where a straightforward reseal would have solved the whole problem for a fraction of the eventual cost.

Edge and Coping Questions From Owners

How do I know if my roof's edge metal is undersized for current wind standards?

We check the fastening pattern and spacing against current code requirements for your building's height and location during an inspection. Many older buildings were built to a lower standard that is now out of date.

Can a coping joint be repaired without replacing the whole coping run?

Often yes, if the failure is isolated to specific joints rather than widespread deterioration across the whole parapet. We assess joint by joint rather than assuming a full replacement is required.

Why did our gutters pull loose during a recent ice storm?

Ice adds significant weight to a gutter system, and hangers spaced or sized for a lighter load can fail under that stress. We reinforce hanger spacing when replacing or repairing storm-damaged gutters.

Is edge metal failure covered by our roof warranty?

That depends on the specific manufacturer warranty and how the original edge metal was installed. We review warranty documentation as part of any edge repair assessment to clarify what is and is not covered.

How often should coping and edge metal be inspected?

At minimum during the standard pre-winter and spring inspection visits, with an additional check after any high-wind event, since edge and coping damage from wind is not always visible from ground level.
