items.name: Emergency Tarp and Dry-In in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Emergency roof stabilization after storm damage in Lansing, with documented, wind-anchored temporary dry-in and a clear path to permanent repair.

sections.html:
Stopping Water Before It Costs More Than the Roof Damage Itself

The first hours after a roof opens up matter more than the eventual repair does. A hole that lets in rain for a full afternoon can soak insulation, damage ceiling grid, and ruin inventory or equipment in a fraction of the time it takes to schedule and complete a permanent fix, which is why emergency dry-in exists as its own service, separate from the repair that follows it.

What Counts as an Emergency on a Lansing Commercial Roof

Active water entry into an occupied space is always an emergency, whether it came from wind damage, a fallen tree limb during a spring thunderstorm, or a membrane failure that finally gave way during a heavy wet snow. Storm-driven damage after high wind events — including the occasional summer derecho that moves through mid-Michigan — often looks worse from the ground than it is, or better than it is, which is why we assess the actual roof condition before deciding what stabilization is needed rather than guessing from a phone description.

Hospitals, schools, plant floors with sensitive equipment, and any building with occupants who cannot simply relocate for a few hours all get priority response, because the cost of delay in those buildings is higher than in a vacant warehouse bay.

What a Tarp-and-Dry-In Actually Involves

This is not a blue tarp thrown over a hole and weighted with cinder blocks. A proper emergency dry-in secures a weatherproof membrane or tarp system anchored to the roof deck or structure in a way that survives wind loading, seals the edges against wind-driven rain, and protects the interior until permanent repair or replacement can be scoped and scheduled. On a low-slope commercial roof, that means paying attention to drainage so the temporary system does not simply redirect water to a different failure point.

We document the damage with photographs before covering it, since that record matters for insurance conversations and for scoping the permanent repair once the emergency phase is over.

Sequencing After the Immediate Stabilization

  • Interior protection — tarping equipment, moving inventory, controlling water flow to a drain point
  • Photo documentation of the damage before and after temporary stabilization
  • A same-week or next-week assessment of what permanent repair the damage actually requires
  • Coordination with the facility team on access, safety, and any areas that need to stay closed off
  • A written scope for the permanent fix once the immediate risk is controlled
  • Removal and disposal of the temporary system once permanent work begins

Skipping the documentation step is one of the most common mistakes we see from owners who try to handle an emergency without a contractor involved from the start — by the time an insurance conversation happens, there is no photographic record of the original damage extent.

Why Speed Matters More Than Perfection in the First Hours

A temporary dry-in does not need to be pretty, it needs to stop water from getting further into the building than it already has. We would rather get a secure, weathertight cover on a damaged roof section within hours than wait to mobilize a full crew for a more polished temporary fix two days later, while the interior keeps absorbing water in the meantime.

That said, a rushed tarp job that blows off in the next wind event creates a second emergency on top of the first one. We anchor for the wind conditions actually forecast for the coming days, rather than for calm weather alone, because Michigan storm systems often arrive in multi-day sequences rather than a single event.

What This Costs Compared to Doing Nothing Until the Permanent Fix

Owners sometimes ask whether the emergency dry-in step is really necessary if the permanent repair is already being scheduled for later that week. The answer is almost always yes, because interior damage compounds every hour water keeps entering the building. Saturated insulation has to be replaced, not dried out. Ceiling grid and tile exposed to repeated water intrusion warps and has to be replaced. Inventory or equipment damage from a preventable delay is a cost that had nothing to do with the original roof problem and everything to do with how long it sat open. The dry-in is what keeps a roof problem from becoming an interior renovation problem.

Coordinating With Facilities and Security Teams

On larger institutional and plant properties, an emergency response involves more than a roofing crew showing up. We coordinate access with security, confirm which areas of the building need to stay closed off during the work, and communicate a realistic timeline to whoever is managing tenant or occupant concerns. That coordination is part of what keeps an emergency response from creating its own disruption on top of the storm damage it is responding to.

Emergency Response Questions From Owners

How fast can you respond to an active roof leak or storm damage?

Active water entry gets same-day priority whenever possible. Response time depends on the scale of the storm event and how many calls are coming in at once, but we do not put an active leak on a normal scheduling queue.

Will a temporary tarp hold up in another storm before permanent repair happens?

A properly anchored dry-in system is built to hold through additional weather, not only the initial event. We check forecast conditions when installing it and reinforce accordingly.

Do you document the damage for our insurance claim?

Yes, we photograph and describe the observed damage as part of the emergency response. That documentation supports your claim conversation, though we do not make promises about how a carrier will rule on it.

How long can a building operate under a temporary dry-in before permanent repair is needed?

It depends on the extent of damage and the season, but a temporary system is not meant to be a long-term solution. We schedule the permanent scope as soon as the emergency phase is stable and the assessment is complete.

What if the damage turns out to be worse than it looked from inside the building?

That is common. We reassess once we are actually on the roof and adjust the stabilization and repair scope based on what we find, rather than what was visible from a ceiling stain alone.

items.name: Insurance Documentation Support in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Factual roof damage documentation for Lansing insurance claims after storms, separating pre-existing wear from new damage without promising a claim outcome.

sections.html:
Documenting Storm Damage for a Claim, Not Promising the Outcome

After a wind, ice, or hail event, the roof documentation an owner has in hand shapes how the insurance conversation goes. We put together that documentation as a contractor, describing what we observed, measured, and repaired, and we are direct about the difference between that role and the role of a public adjuster or attorney.

What We Document and Why It Matters

A claim conversation goes better when the roof condition is documented close to the time of the storm, not weeks later once weather has changed the evidence or temporary repairs have covered up the original damage. We photograph damage before any temporary stabilization, measure the affected area, note the apparent cause where it is evident from the roof, and record what pre-existing conditions were separate from the storm event itself. That last point matters because a carrier will often ask what portion of observed damage predates the claimed event, and having that distinction documented up front is more credible than trying to reconstruct it later.

What This Documentation Cannot Do

We are not a public adjuster and we do not represent an owner's interests in a claim negotiation. We will not tell an owner that documentation guarantees a claim gets approved, and we will not characterize damage as storm-related when the roof condition suggests otherwise. That honesty matters more in the long run than telling an owner what they want to hear in the moment, because a documentation package that overstates the case tends to lose credibility with an adjuster and can hurt the claim rather than help it. We would rather give an owner an accurate, defensible record than a favorable-sounding one that falls apart under review.

What a Documentation Package Includes

  • Dated photographs of the damage, taken before and after any emergency stabilization
  • Measurements of the affected roof area and specific damage locations
  • A description of the apparent cause based on roof condition and known storm activity
  • Notes distinguishing storm-related damage from pre-existing wear where both are present
  • An itemized repair or replacement scope with cost estimates for the recommended work
  • A copy of any prior inspection reports on file, for comparison against the pre-storm condition

Having a prior inspection report on file is one of the most valuable things an owner can have going into a claim conversation, because it establishes what condition the roof was in before the storm, which is exactly what a carrier wants to know.

Working Alongside an Adjuster

When a carrier's adjuster comes out to inspect the roof, we can meet them on site to walk through what we found and answer technical questions about the damage and the recommended scope. We present the facts as we documented them and let the adjuster reach their own conclusion, rather than steering the conversation toward a predetermined outcome. If the adjuster's assessment differs from ours on a specific point, we can explain our reasoning, but the decision on the claim itself is between the owner and the carrier.

Documentation for Repeat Events

Michigan's storm calendar means some properties file more than one weather-related claim in a given year — an ice storm in January, thunderstorm wind damage in May. When that happens, having distinct documentation for each event, rather than one running file that blends damage from separate storms together, matters for how cleanly each claim can be evaluated. We date and separate our documentation by event specifically so an owner is never trying to explain to an adjuster which damage came from which storm months after the fact.

Timing Matters More Than Owners Expect

Most policies have a window for filing a claim after a storm event, and roof damage that is not documented promptly gets harder to substantiate as time passes and weather continues to affect the roof. We recommend getting a documentation visit scheduled within days of a significant storm, even if the full repair scope will take longer to finalize, because the documentation itself is time-sensitive in a way the repair decision is not. Waiting until the repair budget is fully approved before documenting the damage is one of the more common mistakes we see, and it is an avoidable one.

Insurance Documentation Questions From Owners

Can you guarantee our claim will be approved if you document the damage?

No, and we would be misleading you if we said otherwise. We document what we observe accurately and factually; the claim decision belongs to your carrier.

Do you charge separately for insurance documentation visits?

That depends on the scope of the visit and whether it is combined with an emergency response or scheduled inspection. We are upfront about cost before the visit happens.

Should we call you or our insurance company first after storm damage?

Either can happen first, but getting documentation started quickly matters regardless of order. If there is active water entry, stabilizing that takes priority over which call happens first.

What if the adjuster disagrees with our contractor's assessment?

That happens sometimes. We can walk the adjuster through our reasoning and the physical evidence, but the final claim determination is a conversation between you and your carrier, not something we can override.

Can old inspection reports help with a new storm claim?

Yes, significantly. A prior report establishes baseline roof condition before the storm, which helps distinguish new storm damage from pre-existing wear in the current claim conversation.

items.name: Roofing for Property Management Portfolios in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Standing roof vendor services for property managers overseeing office, retail, and multifamily buildings from Delta Township to Okemos, Michigan.

sections.html:
One Roofer Across a Multi-Building Portfolio Instead of Ten

Property managers around Lansing typically oversee roofs scattered from Delta Township to Okemos: office parks, strip retail centers, and apartment communities that each have their own age, system type, and owner. We work as a standing vendor across a portfolio rather than getting called in fresh every time one building has a problem.

Standardizing Roof Condition Data Across a Portfolio

Comparing roofs across a portfolio is difficult when every inspection report looks different because a different vendor wrote it. We provide consistent inspection reports, photos, and remaining-life estimates across every property we cover, in the same format every time, so a property manager can line them up side by side and see which building actually needs the next dollar of capital spend.

We also track roof age and system type per building over time, which turns replacement planning into something proactive instead of a reaction to whichever roof leaks first.

This matters most at handoff, when a portfolio changes hands between owners or a new asset manager takes over a set of properties. A clean, dated roof history for every building saves the incoming team from starting due diligence with no record at all.

Budgeting Roof Capex Across Ownership Groups and Fiscal Years

Many property management firms manage buildings on behalf of several different ownership groups, each running its own reserve fund and fiscal calendar. We phase proposals to match each owner's specific capital planning cycle rather than sending a portfolio-wide number that doesn't map to how any one owner actually budgets.

Our repair, recover, and replacement recommendations get sized to what a given reserve fund can reasonably absorb in the current year, with the longer-term plan laid out separately so the owner can see what's coming without being asked to fund all of it at once.

We also flag when a repair is close enough to the cost of a recover that spending the extra money now avoids paying for the same square footage twice within a few years. That comparison is easy to lose track of across a large portfolio without someone tracking it building by building.

Tenant Coordination Without the Property Manager in the Middle of Every Call

Office parks and strip retail buildings have tenants who call the property manager the moment water shows up, and that call usually lands on the PM's desk regardless of whether it's actually the PM's job to schedule the repair. We coordinate access and scheduling directly with tenants once a work order is approved, keeping the property manager informed without making them the go-between on every detail.

A leak in one retail unit sometimes requires roof access directly above a neighboring tenant's space. We manage that coordination carefully, including scheduling around that tenant's business hours, so a repair for one unit doesn't become a disruption for the one next to it.

Preventive Maintenance as a Budget Tool

Seasonal inspection visits in spring and fall catch clogged drains, lifting seams, and early flashing failures before they turn into an emergency call, which is the whole point of a maintenance program rather than a nice-to-have add-on. Every issue we find during a scheduled visit is one that didn't happen at 2 a.m. during a storm.

Fall inspections matter in particular here, given how hard Michigan's freeze-thaw winters are on a roof that's already carrying a small problem. A clogged drain that would be a minor nuisance in August becomes ponding ice and a split seam by February if nobody catches it first.

Reporting That Fits How Owners Actually Review Performance

Some ownership groups want a detailed line-item breakdown of every repair; others want a one-page summary tied to the reserve study. We ask up front how each owner wants information presented and format our reports to match, rather than sending the same document to every stakeholder regardless of what they actually use it for.

When a property changes ownership mid-contract, we carry that same reporting history forward for the new owner, so the transition doesn't reset the roof record back to zero on day one. We've found that this continuity is often what separates a smooth ownership handoff from one where the incoming team spends its first quarter just figuring out what condition each roof is actually in.

  • Single- and multi-tenant office buildings
  • Neighborhood and strip retail centers
  • Garden-style apartment and multifamily buildings
  • Self-storage facility roofs
  • Mixed-use buildings with ground-floor retail

Common Questions From Portfolio and Asset Managers

Can you provide standardized inspection reports across every property we manage?

Yes, that consistency is the main reason firms bring us on as a standing vendor rather than calling around per building.

Do you bill each property separately when we manage buildings for different owners?

Yes. We invoice by property and owner so each reserve fund only carries its own building's costs.

How quickly can you respond to a leak call from a tenant at one of our properties?

Response time depends on severity, but active leaks get prioritized the same day whenever possible, with a temporary fix in place while the permanent repair gets scheduled.

Can a preventive maintenance program actually lower our annual roof spend?

In most portfolios, yes, because catching small issues during a scheduled visit is consistently cheaper than an emergency call after a leak has already caused interior damage.

Will you coordinate scheduling directly with our tenants instead of routing everything through us?

Yes, once a work order is approved we'll handle direct scheduling with the tenant and keep you updated, rather than making you the relay for every detail.

items.name: Manufacturing Plant Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof repair and replacement for supplier plants, warehouses, and processing facilities along the Lansing area's auto manufacturing corridor.

sections.html:
Roofing Over the Supplier Shops and Plants Around Lansing's Auto Industry

Two General Motors assembly plants anchor a supplier base that fills industrial parks along US-127, I-96, and I-69, from Delta Township and DeWitt Township out to Grand Ledge and Watertown Township. We work on the stamping shops, tooling plants, warehouses, and processing facilities that fill out that supply chain and the industrial buildings around it, not the assembly plants themselves.

A Supply Chain Stacked Along US-127, I-96, and I-69

The concentration of tier-one and tier-two automotive suppliers around Lansing exists because of proximity to the assembly plants, and that proximity put a lot of low-slope industrial roof square footage up along the same highway corridors over the past few decades. DeWitt Township's US-127 corridor, the industrial parks off I-96 in Watertown Township and Webberville, and the plants around Grand Ledge and Charlotte all carry this mix of stamping, molding, and logistics buildings.

Outside the auto supply chain, the region has its own processing and agribusiness plants that carry the same kind of roof, including large dairy and food processing operations around St. Johns and container manufacturing facilities near Mason. The roof problems on these buildings look similar regardless of what the plant makes: heavy equipment loads, constant penetrations, and very little tolerance for downtime.

Roof Penetrations for Process Equipment That Never Stops

Manufacturing roofs accumulate curbs and penetrations over years of operational changes: dust collection systems, paint booth exhaust, compressor room vents, and rooftop units added to support new equipment on the floor below. Each one of those is a flashing detail that has to move with the roof membrane instead of tearing it, and on older systems these are usually the first place a leak starts.

We re-flash and re-seal these penetrations without shutting the line down underneath them wherever the plant's safety protocols allow it, working from outside access and coordinating hot work permits where coatings or solvents nearby make that a real constraint. A plant floor manager doesn't want to hear that a roof repair requires stopping production, and in most cases it doesn't have to.

Coatings and Chemical Exposure From Plant Operations

Exhaust from paint lines, welding smoke, and solvent vapor settle on a roof membrane over time and degrade certain systems faster than ordinary UV and weather exposure would on their own. This shows up as a membrane that looks fine from the ground but is losing flexibility and seam strength years ahead of schedule.

Silicone and acrylic roof coatings hold up better against this kind of chemical exposure than an uncoated membrane, and we recommend them specifically for buildings where plant exhaust is part of the roof's daily environment, not as a blanket upsell on every job.

Scheduling Around Shift Production, Not Business Hours

Many of the plants we work around run multiple shifts, and some run close to around the clock. Roof access, material staging, and noise all have to work around shift change, forklift traffic on the yard below, and whatever quiet hours the plant enforces for the shift that's trying to sleep during the day.

We ask for the plant's production schedule before we ever propose a start date, and we build crane and material delivery windows around it instead of assuming a roof crew has the run of the site whenever it wants it.

  • Stamping and tooling shops
  • Injection molding plants
  • Warehouse and distribution buildings supporting the supply chain
  • Food, dairy, and container processing plants
  • Agribusiness and ag-equipment service buildings
  • Cold storage and logistics facilities

What a Roof Failure Costs a Plant Floor

Water reaching an electrical panel, a conveyor line, or raw material stock on a plant floor costs a lot more than the roof section that let it in, and the lost production time from an unplanned shutdown almost always outweighs the repair bill. A roof problem on a manufacturing building is rarely just a roof problem by the time it's noticed.

When a leak does hit a plant floor, we prioritize an emergency tarp or dry-in over anything else, specifically to get the line back to normal operating condition while the permanent repair gets scoped and scheduled properly instead of rushed.

Plant Manager and Facilities Engineer Questions

Do you work around a plant that runs multiple shifts?

Yes. We ask for the production and shift schedule up front and set crew hours, material delivery, and equipment noise around it rather than assuming standard daytime access.

Can you flash around dust collectors, exhaust stacks, and rooftop unit curbs without shutting a line down?

In most cases, yes. We work from outside the curb wherever access allows it and coordinate with plant safety staff on any hot work permit the job requires.

What roof coatings hold up best against solvent and welding smoke exposure?

Silicone coatings generally hold up best against that kind of chemical exposure. We assess what's actually settling on the roof before recommending a specific system.

How fast can you respond with a temporary tarp if a leak hits a plant floor?

We treat plant floor leaks as an emergency call and prioritize dry-in over a full assessment, since equipment and material below the roof are usually more time-sensitive than the roof itself.

Do you carry the safety documentation required to get onto a manufacturing site?

We provide standard insurance certificates and safety documentation on request, and we follow whatever site-specific orientation or badge process a plant requires before crews are allowed on the property.

items.name: Medical Facility Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof repair and maintenance for hospitals, surgery centers, and medical office buildings across the Lansing, East Lansing, and Okemos medical corridor.

sections.html:
Roof Work That Doesn't Interrupt Patient Care

Sparrow and McLaren Greater Lansing operate the region's largest hospital campuses, with surgery centers and medical office buildings spread through Okemos, East Lansing, and Delta Township. Healthcare roofs answer to infection control and continuous operation before anything else, and that changes how nearly every part of the job gets done.

Infection Control Requirements Change How We Work the Roof

Dust and debris containment matters directly above surgery suites, sterile processing areas, and imaging equipment, and we plan containment before work starts near any of those spaces, not after someone raises a concern. That planning happens in coordination with the facility's infection control staff, following the containment protocols the building already has in place.

Roof intake locations for HVAC systems serving patient care areas get special attention. We sequence work to stay away from active air intakes wherever possible, specifically to avoid pulling roofing debris into a system that's supplying air to a patient floor.

We also keep crews briefed on which zones of a roof sit above sensitive spaces before work starts each day, rather than relying on what was true at the start of the project. A rooftop unit added since the last time we were on site can move that line, so we confirm it rather than working from an outdated mental map of the building below.

Surgery Centers and Medical Office Buildings Can't Close

A surgical center or medical office building has patients scheduled weeks in advance, unlike an empty retail box that can sit closed during a renovation. We phase roof sections and schedule the loudest work outside procedure hours whenever the building's operating schedule allows it.

Vibration and noise limits near exam rooms and recovery areas are real constraints, not a courtesy. We confirm what equipment we can and can't run during business hours with facilities staff before a project starts, rather than finding out mid-project that a compressor is too loud for the space below.

Rooftop Mechanical Density on Hospital and Clinic Buildings

Medical buildings carry a heavier rooftop mechanical load than a typical office building: dedicated air handlers, backup generators, and medical gas vents, all in addition to standard rooftop units. Every one of those is a penetration, and more penetrations means more places for flashing to fail first.

During routine maintenance visits, we prioritize inspection around this equipment specifically because it carries more risk than an open field section of membrane, and catching a small flashing failure here matters more than it would on a lower-stakes building.

Backup generator exhaust and medical gas vent penetrations also see more thermal cycling than a standard curb, since some of that equipment runs intermittently rather than at a constant temperature. We factor that into how often we recommend those specific details get checked.

Emergency Response When a Leak Threatens Equipment or a Procedure Room

Water above an operating room, an imaging suite, or a server closet is never a wait-a-week problem. Emergency tarp and dry-in response protects equipment that often costs far more than the roof section causing the leak, and we treat these calls accordingly.

We coordinate access directly with facilities staff, who usually need to protect or relocate what's below the affected area before a roof crew is even on site, and we build that sequencing into how fast we can actually start work.

Documentation for Accreditation and Facility Records

Healthcare facilities keep detailed maintenance records for accreditation reviews, and roof work is part of that file. We provide dated inspection reports and repair documentation in a format that slots directly into a facility's existing recordkeeping rather than requiring staff to reformat what we hand over.

We also keep a running history of every visit to a given facility, so if a surveyor asks about the last roof inspection date or the status of an open repair item, the facilities team has an answer on hand instead of digging through old files. That history also makes it easier to spot a pattern, like the same rooftop unit curb needing attention every winter, instead of treating each visit as an isolated event. Over several seasons that record becomes useful on its own, telling a facilities team which sections of roof are aging faster than the rest of the building around them.

  • Hospital wings and connector roofs
  • Ambulatory surgery centers
  • Medical office buildings
  • Urgent care and outpatient clinics
  • Imaging center roofs with heavy rooftop mechanical loads

Questions From Facilities and Infection Control Staff

Do you follow infection control containment protocols when working near air intakes?

Yes. We coordinate directly with facility infection control staff before starting work near any active intake serving patient care areas.

Can roof work be scheduled entirely outside patient procedure hours?

In most cases, yes, particularly for the loudest phases of a project. We confirm the facility's procedure schedule before setting work hours.

How do you handle the extra rooftop penetrations around medical gas and generator equipment?

We give this equipment priority attention during inspection since it represents more penetrations and more risk than a standard rooftop unit array.

What's your response time if a leak threatens equipment in an imaging suite?

We treat this as an emergency call and prioritize immediate tarp and dry-in response over a full assessment, given what's typically at risk below the roof.

Will your crew coordinate directly with our facilities department before mobilizing?

Yes, every time. Facilities staff usually need to prepare the space below before we start, and we build that lead time into our schedule.

items.name: School and Campus Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof replacement and repair for K-12 schools, Lansing Community College, and MSU-area campus buildings, sequenced around the academic calendar.

sections.html:
Roof Work Timed to the Academic Calendar, Not the Other Way Around

Michigan State University in East Lansing, Lansing Community College downtown, and school districts spread across Lansing, East Lansing, Okemos, Holt, Waverly, and DeWitt put a large share of the region's flat roof square footage on education campuses. Summer break is the real construction season for most of this building stock, and the schedule doesn't move for weather delays or a slow material order.

Summer Is a Deadline, Not a Suggestion

The window between the last day of school and the first day back in the fall is fixed, and a roof project that isn't watertight when students and staff return creates problems that go well beyond an unfinished punch list. We plan tear-off and replacement sequencing specifically to hit that date, working backward from the first day of school rather than forward from a contract signing.

A project that slips into the school year causes real friction with the district. Roof crews working over occupied classrooms create noise, dust, and safety concerns that most districts won't accept once students are back in the building, so a phasing plan that finishes on time matters more here than on almost any other type of project we handle.

We order material early specifically to protect that date. A membrane or insulation package that's back-ordered into July eats directly into the installation window, so we confirm availability before a district commits to a summer start date, not after.

Campus Buildings With Roofs That Never Go Empty

MSU's campus doesn't empty out the way a K-12 building does. Dormitories, dining halls, and athletic facilities run summer camps, conferences, and staff operations through the break, which means a roof project on campus needs closer coordination with building schedules than a district that's simply closed for the summer.

We sequence these projects section by section, keeping occupied wings dry and functional while work moves across the rest of the roof, rather than treating the whole building as available at once.

Move-in weekend and the start of fall classes are hard stops on a campus schedule the way the first bell is for a K-12 building. We plan backward from those dates the same way, and we flag early if a project's scope is putting a campus deadline at risk.

Aging Flat Roofs on Mid-Century School Buildings

A lot of the school building stock in the districts around Lansing dates to additions built between the 1950s and 1970s, many of them carrying built-up or modified bitumen roofs that are well past a reasonable service life. Ponding water and saturated insulation are common findings on these older low-slope sections.

Before recommending a full replacement, we run a moisture survey to find out how much of the insulation underneath is actually wet. A roof that tests dry in most areas might only need a recover in those sections, and that distinction matters a lot to a district working within a fixed capital budget.

Budgeting Roof Replacement Against a District's Bond Cycle

Most district capital projects, including roof replacement, get funded through a bond proposal that residents vote on, and roof work often gets bundled into a larger package alongside HVAC or security upgrades. We provide condition assessments districts can use to prioritize which roof sections go into a bond scope and which can wait for the next cycle.

Districts that phase a bond over several buildings need to know which roof is closest to failure and which one has a few more seasons left. We rank sections by remaining life and risk rather than by square footage alone, so limited bond dollars go where they matter most first.

Coordinating With Facilities Staff Who Wear Several Hats

A district facilities director is often managing HVAC, grounds, and security alongside the roof, with far less bandwidth than a dedicated capital projects office. We keep communication direct and infrequent rather than burying a small facilities team in weekly status calls they don't have time for.

We also flag issues that affect other trades working the same summer window, like a roof drain tie-in that needs to happen before a paving crew closes off a section of parking lot, so scheduling conflicts get caught before they cost a week.

  • K-12 elementary and middle school buildings
  • High school gymnasium and fieldhouse roofs
  • District administration and bus garage buildings
  • Community college classroom and lab buildings
  • University dormitory and dining hall roofs
  • Campus athletic facility roofs

What District Facilities Staff and Campus Planners Ask

Can a full roof replacement realistically happen in a single summer break?

For most single-building sections, yes, if the project is scoped and material ordered well ahead of the break. Larger campuses or multiple buildings sometimes need a phased multi-summer plan.

How do you handle noise and dust restrictions on an occupied campus building?

We schedule the loudest work during the hours the building allows and use containment where equipment or debris could affect indoor air quality in occupied wings.

What's involved in a moisture survey before we commit to full replacement?

We scan the roof to identify areas of wet insulation, then map those findings against the roof so a district knows exactly which sections need replacement and which can be recovered.

Can you provide documentation districts need for a bond-funded capital project?

Yes. We provide condition assessments and cost estimates in a format that supports a bond package or a board presentation.

Do you work weekends or nights to avoid disrupting summer camps and events on campus?

We can, and we coordinate that schedule directly with campus facilities staff around whatever camps, conferences, or events are running during the work window.

items.name: Retail Chain Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof repair and replacement for retail chains and shopping centers from Meridian Mall to the Cedar Street big-box corridor in greater Lansing.

sections.html:
Keeping Stores Open While the Roof Above Them Gets Fixed

From Meridian Mall in Okemos to Eastwood Towne Center and the big-box corridor along Cedar Street and Martin Luther King Boulevard, retail roofs here answer to one rule above the rest: the doors stay open. We work the schedule around the store's hours, not the other way around.

Roof Work That Never Shows Up on the Sales Floor

Overnight and off-hours scheduling is standard for big-box and mall-anchor stores, and we stage material and equipment away from customer parking and entrances so a shopper walking in has no idea a roof project is underway above them. Loading docks and back-of-house access get used first, before customer-facing areas ever come into play.

Sound and vibration limits during business hours matter more at some stores than others, particularly retailers with quiet zones or a shared wall with another tenant. We confirm those limits with store management before scheduling any work that runs during open hours.

We also plan around a store's own calendar rather than treating every week the same. A back-to-school push at a big-box store or a holiday buildup at a mall anchor is the wrong week to have a crew and a dumpster in the parking lot, and we schedule around those windows whenever a project's timeline gives us room to.

One Point of Contact for a Chain With Multiple Locations

A retail chain operator managing several stores across the region generally wants one roofer who already knows every location's roof history, not a different vendor called in fresh at each address. We track system type, install age, and warranty status per location so a regional facilities manager isn't starting from zero every time a store needs attention.

Proposals come back in the same standardized format across every store, which lets a regional manager compare locations directly and prioritize budget the same way every time instead of reading five differently formatted bids.

Big-Box Roof Loads From Rooftop Signage and HVAC

Large-format retail roofs carry a heavy count of rooftop HVAC units plus illuminated pylon and rooftop signage structures, and every curb and penetration tied to that equipment is a spot we check first during inspection. These roofs fail at the equipment interfaces long before the open field of membrane shows any wear.

Reflective, cool-roof coatings come up often on these buildings, and the benefit goes beyond the membrane itself. A more reflective roof surface also reduces in-store HVAC load during summer months, which shows up on the utility bill as well as the roof's condition.

Storm Response Across Multiple Store Locations at Once

A single hail or wind event can hit every store in a chain's regional footprint on the same afternoon, and we triage by which roofs show active leaks first, mobilizing tarp crews to protect merchandise at the locations that need it most urgently. Waiting to assess every store in order arriving at each in turn wastes time a leaking roof doesn't have.

We provide a single consolidated damage report covering every affected location instead of five separate one-off reports, which is what a regional facilities team actually needs to manage a multi-store claim or repair plan.

Planning Roof Replacement Around a Remodel or Refresh Cycle

Chains often tie roof replacement to a broader store remodel or refresh cycle rather than replacing a roof in isolation. When we know a remodel is coming, we plan the roof scope to avoid conflicts with new rooftop equipment, updated signage, or exterior work happening on the same schedule, so the two projects don't end up working against each other.

We also flag when a remodel's added rooftop equipment load changes what the existing roof structure can support, since a new HVAC package or additional signage curb sometimes calls for a structural check before it goes up, beyond the flashing detail alone. Catching that early keeps the roof scope and the remodel scope on the same timeline instead of one holding up the other.

  • Enclosed mall anchor and inline stores
  • Big-box and warehouse-club buildings
  • Grocery-anchored strip centers
  • Freestanding pad-site retail
  • Power center and lifestyle center storefronts

What Regional Facilities Managers Want to Know

Can roof work happen entirely after store hours?

Yes, in most cases. We schedule the disruptive phases overnight and confirm any exceptions with store management ahead of time.

Do you track roof history and warranty status separately for each of our locations?

Yes. We keep system type, install date, and warranty information on file per address so we're not starting from scratch every time a store calls.

How do you prioritize response when a storm hits multiple stores at once?

We triage by severity, sending crews first to locations with active leaks or structural concerns, then working through the rest of the affected footprint.

Can you protect merchandise below the roof deck while repairs are underway?

Emergency tarp and dry-in work is aimed specifically at protecting what's below the roof, and we coordinate with store staff on covering or relocating merchandise if needed. For grocery-anchored centers, that coordination often has to happen fast, since spoiled inventory below a leak is a bigger loss than the roof repair itself.

Will you send one consolidated report across all our locations instead of separate ones per store?

Yes, that's the standard format we use for chain accounts, since it's what a regional team actually needs to manage a multi-location repair plan.

items.name: Government & Public-Sector Roofing in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof inspection, repair, and replacement for state, county, and city buildings in Lansing, priced and scheduled against public budget cycles.

sections.html:
Roofing State, County, and City Buildings Through Michigan's Budget Calendar

Lansing is the state capital, which means the local roof stock includes state office buildings, an Ingham County courthouse and annex complex, city fire and police stations, and every Secretary of State branch and public library in between. Public roofs answer to a bid file and a budget line before they answer to anything else, and the freeze-thaw winters here are hard on the flat and low-slope systems that cover most of them.

The Capital Outlay Cycle Sets the Real Timeline

State building projects in Michigan move through a capital outlay process tied to multi-year appropriation cycles, not a phone call and a signature. A roof that needs replacing on a state office building along Ottawa or Allegan Street often shows up in a facilities condition report years before funding is approved, and the roofer who gets the work is the one who can produce a defensible condition assessment early, not the one who shows up after a leak.

County and city capital budgets move faster than the state's but still run on an annual or biennial planning calendar. Ingham County and the City of Lansing both work from a capital improvement plan that ranks projects by urgency and available funding, and a roof section that slips off this year's list has to wait for next year's unless it becomes an emergency.

We build our proposals to fit inside that process instead of around it. That means remaining-life estimates broken out by roof section, unit pricing that survives a line-item budget review, and phasing options that let a facilities director fund a project across two fiscal years if that's what it takes to get approved.

Bid Packages, Unit Pricing, and Prevailing Wage Documentation

Public agencies solicit formal bids, and the roofing scope in that bid package has to hold up against three or four competing proposals reviewed by staff who may not be roofing specialists. We write scope documents with manufacturer data sheets, wind uplift ratings, and insulation R-values spelled out, so the comparison between bidders is actually apples to apples.

Projects that require prevailing wage rate documentation get payroll records and certified wage forms submitted on the schedule the contracting agency sets, not chased down after the fact. A vague scope is where public roof projects go wrong. Change orders on a public contract are harder to approve than on a private job because the extra money usually has to go back through the same budget process that approved the original number, so we scope the work tight before it ever goes out to bid.

Freeze-Thaw Stress on Flat Roofs Across the Capitol Complex

Michigan winters put flat and low-slope membrane through repeated freeze-thaw cycling, and older built-up and modified bitumen systems on state and county buildings downtown show the wear from decades of it. Ponding water that sits on an aging roof freezes, expands, and works seams and flashing loose faster than a system with proper positive drainage.

Ice damming at parapet walls and roof drains is the other recurring issue on this building stock. When meltwater backs up behind an ice ridge and refreezes, it finds every weak seam and pushes water into the deck below, which on a records room, a server closet, or a courtroom is a much bigger problem than the roof repair itself.

Buildings That Cannot Close to the Public

A Secretary of State branch office, a courthouse, and a fire or police station cannot shut their doors while a roof gets replaced. We phase these projects section by section, staging material and equipment away from public entrances and scheduling the loudest work outside peak counter hours where the building's operations allow it.

Secured buildings add another layer of coordination. A courthouse or a Michigan State Police post may require credential checks, escorted access, or advance notice before a crew can get on the roof at all, and we build that lead time into the schedule instead of treating it as a surprise the morning work is supposed to start.

  • State office buildings and agency headquarters
  • County courthouse and administrative annex buildings
  • City fire and police stations
  • Secretary of State branch offices
  • Public libraries
  • Senior and community centers
  • County health department and human services buildings

What a Deferred Roof Costs a Public Budget

A leak that reaches records storage, IT infrastructure, or a courtroom costs far more to fix than the roof section that let the water in. Emergency repairs pulled from a contingency line also tend to cost more per square foot than the same work planned and bid a year ahead, because there's no time to competitively price it.

We document roof condition on a recurring basis specifically so a facilities director has the paperwork to request capital funding before a system fails, not after. A dated inspection report with photos and remaining-life estimates is what turns a roof section from a hypothetical future problem into a line item a budget committee can actually approve.

What Facilities Directors and Procurement Staff Ask Us

Can you provide unit pricing broken into repair, recover, and replace line items for a board or council vote?

Yes. We separate those options with individual unit costs so a facilities director or council member can compare the near-term and long-term price difference in the same document.

Do you have experience working within a multi-year capital outlay funding schedule?

Yes. We phase scope and pricing to match how state, county, and city capital budgets actually get approved, including splitting a project across fiscal years when that's the only way it gets funded.

How do you handle site access at secured buildings like a courthouse or a state police post?

We coordinate credential checks, escort requirements, and any advance notice the building requires before scheduling crews, so access isn't a surprise on the day work is supposed to start.

What happens when a leak shows up mid-fiscal-year with no line item budgeted?

We stabilize the roof first with a tarp or emergency patch, then provide the documentation needed to request contingency funding or move the repair into next year's capital plan.

Can roof work be scheduled around a legislative session or court calendar?

Yes. We ask about session dates, docket schedules, and any public events before setting a start date, and we phase noisy work around them where the building's use allows it.

items.name: Roofing Subcontractor Services in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof bidding, dry-in, and closeout documentation for general contractors handling new construction, tenant build-outs, and additions around Lansing.

sections.html:
Roofing Scope for General Contractors Building in the Capital Region

New construction and tenant build-out work has been steady around Lansing, tied to state government facility projects, development near Michigan State University, and expansion among the manufacturers along the US-127 and I-96 corridors. We sub roof scope on new construction, additions, and tenant improvement projects for general contractors who need a bid they can trust and a schedule they can build around.

Bidding Roof Scope From Plans, Not a Walkthrough

We price roof scope directly from the plan set and the relevant spec division, not from a rough guess at square footage. That means reading the wind uplift requirements, insulation values, and membrane specification called out in the documents and pricing to that standard, so the number we hand a GC's estimator holds up through award and doesn't turn into a change order later.

When the spec allows an alternate, we'll offer one, whether that's a TPO alternate to a specified PVC system or a different insulation package that hits the same R-value at a better price. We flag these as options in the bid, not as a substitution decided after the contract is signed, so the GC and owner can make that call with full information.

We also flag conflicts between the roof plan and the structural or mechanical drawings before bid day, not after mobilization. A rooftop unit schedule that doesn't match the curb layout on the roof plan, or a parapet detail that doesn't account for a planned rooftop screen wall, is much cheaper to fix on paper than after material is ordered.

Sequencing Roof Work Into a Tight Construction Schedule

Dry-in date drives everything that follows it. Framing crews, mechanical rough-in, and drywall all wait on a watertight roof, and a late dry-in cascades into every trade scheduled behind it. We coordinate directly with the GC's superintendent on deck completion so the roof crew mobilizes the day it can, not a week after.

Michigan's fall weather window adds real pressure on projects trying to get dried in before the first hard freeze. We build that seasonal deadline into our schedule commitments up front rather than discovering it mid-project, and we'll flag early if a project's overall schedule is putting the roof date at risk.

We also track material lead times separately from labor scheduling. A specified membrane or insulation package on backorder is a scheduling risk a GC needs to know about at bid time, not two weeks before the deck is ready, so we confirm availability before committing to a dry-in date on the schedule.

Tenant Build-Outs and Rooftop Unit Coordination

Tenant improvement projects almost always need new curb cuts for rooftop HVAC units, and we coordinate directly with the mechanical sub on exact penetration locations and curb heights before equipment gets set, rather than flashing around a unit that's already in place. Getting that sequence right the first time avoids a second mobilization.

Multiple trades working on one roof creates a warranty question most GCs don't think about until it comes up: manufacturer warranties usually require the roofer of record to inspect and properly flash any penetration another trade cuts after substantial completion. We spell this out in our scope so it's clear whose responsibility it is if someone else needs to cut the roof later.

Punch List and Warranty Documentation GCs Actually Need

Closeout paperwork holds up final payment more often than it should. We provide manufacturer warranty registration, as-built roof documentation, and a punch list response on the timeline the GC's closeout schedule requires, not weeks after the rest of the project has already closed out.

We turn punch list items around fast because we know what a slow response actually costs a GC: retainage held longer than it needs to be, and an owner who judges the whole project by whichever trade is still on the list. A roof punch item sitting open for a month reflects on the GC long after our crew has left the site.

  • Ground-up warehouse and distribution shells
  • Medical office and retail tenant build-outs
  • School addition and renovation projects
  • Multifamily and mixed-use new construction
  • Manufacturing and industrial expansion projects

What a Late or Wrong Roof Bid Costs a GC

An underbid roof package forces a change order that eats into a GC's margin and puts a strain on the owner relationship right when a project needs that relationship intact. A missed dry-in date is worse: every trade scheduled behind the roof slides with it, and that slippage is hard to make up on the back end of a project.

We requalify scope against the actual project spec before committing a price, not after the contract is signed, specifically to avoid handing a GC a number that doesn't survive the real building conditions.

Questions Estimators and PMs Ask Before They Sub Us In

Can you turn a roof bid from a plan set within a normal estimating deadline?

Yes, in most cases within the standard bid window GCs work with, assuming the drawing set and spec sections are complete.

Do you carry the insurance and bonding capacity most GCs require of subs?

We carry standard contractor insurance and can provide documentation for a GC's prequalification process, and we'll discuss bonding on a project-specific basis.

How do you handle rooftop unit curb coordination with the mechanical sub?

We confirm exact curb locations and heights with the mechanical sub before setting any equipment, so flashing goes in correctly the first time instead of being retrofit around a unit already in place.

What's your lead time to mobilize once we release the roof package?

Lead time depends on material availability and current crew commitments, but we give a firm mobilization date at contract signing and flag it immediately if anything threatens that date.

Will you warranty flashing work around penetrations other trades cut later?

We'll inspect and flash any penetration cut after our work is complete, but that scope and its warranty terms need to be agreed on up front, not assumed after the fact.

items.name: Storm Damage Roof Documentation in Lansing, MI
items.meta: Roof storm damage assessment, emergency tarping, and photo documentation for insurance claims on commercial buildings across greater Lansing, Michigan.

sections.html:
Roof Documentation That Holds Up Through the Claims Process

Mid-Michigan takes spring and summer straight-line wind and hail from storm cells moving through the region, and it takes ice damming from freeze-thaw cycling every winter. We document roof damage the way an adjuster needs to see it, and we don't promise a claim outcome, because that isn't ours to promise.

What Actually Damages a Flat Roof Here

Hail bruises and sometimes fractures membrane surfaces, particularly on older systems that have already lost some flexibility. Wind uplift shows up first at parapet edges and around rooftop unit curbs, where the membrane is most exposed to being lifted and torn. Winter brings a different problem: ice dams and ponding water that freezes and refreezes at seams and drains until something splits.

Not every roof problem an owner notices after a storm is actually storm damage. Adjusters draw a hard line between storm-caused damage and ordinary wear that happened to get noticed after a storm, and we make that same distinction before we ever describe something as storm-related in our documentation.

Spring is the season we see the most call volume, when fast-moving storm cells bring both hail and straight-line wind through mid-Michigan within the same afternoon. A roof can take both kinds of damage in a single event, which is part of why the documentation has to be specific about what happened where.

Documentation Before the Adjuster Ever Walks the Roof

We take dated photographs, run a moisture survey where water intrusion is suspected, and sketch the affected area against the specific storm date being claimed. This record exists independent of any insurance company and stands on its own regardless of how the claim proceeds.

We also provide a written scope of damage and repair estimate that an adjuster can compare directly against their own findings, which speeds up the review process more than a vague description of what happened ever does.

Test cuts help settle disputes over hidden damage, particularly on a membrane that looks intact from above but has water trapped in the insulation below. We only pull a test cut where the evidence points to it, and we document the finding either way, whether or not it supports a bigger claim.

Emergency Tarp and Dry-In While the Claim Moves

A claims review can take weeks, and interior damage keeps compounding the entire time a roof stays open to weather. We tarp and dry-in the affected area immediately regardless of where the claim stands, because protecting the building comes first.

This also protects the claim itself. Ongoing, uncontrolled water intrusion after initial damage complicates a coverage review in ways that a documented, mitigated roof does not.

Working With the Building Owner's Adjuster and Public Adjuster

We provide the technical roofing information both a carrier's adjuster and any public adjuster the owner has hired will need, but we don't represent either side in the negotiation. Our job is documenting conditions on the roof accurately.

Our repair or replacement recommendation is grounded in the actual damage we find, not in whatever scope would maximize a claim payout. That's the only way our documentation holds up if a claim gets questioned later.

Repair Versus Full Replacement After a Storm

A storm doesn't always mean the whole roof needs to come off. When damage is limited to a specific area, a targeted repair can be the right call, and we say so even when a full replacement would be the bigger job for us. When damage is widespread across an aging system, we explain why a repair-only approach would likely fail again within a season or two, so the owner can weigh that against the cost difference with real information.

Coverage decisions sometimes hinge on this exact distinction, since a carrier reviewing a claim wants to know whether they're paying to fix storm damage or to upgrade an aging roof that was already near replacement. We keep those two categories separate in our documentation so the claim reflects what the storm actually did, and so an owner isn't surprised later by a coverage dispute over pre-existing wear that got mixed into the original scope.

  • Wind-lifted membrane and flashing at parapet edges
  • Hail-bruised or fractured membrane surfaces
  • Ice-dam splits at drains and field seams
  • Rooftop unit curb and duct damage from wind
  • Tree limb and wind-borne debris punctures

What Owners Ask When a Claim Is in Process

Will you guarantee my claim gets approved?

No, and we won't tell you otherwise. We document conditions accurately and provide the technical information a carrier needs; the coverage decision belongs to the insurance company.

How fast can you get a tarp on the roof after a storm?

Active leaks get treated as an emergency, and we prioritize getting a temporary tarp or dry-in in place as fast as crew and material availability allow.

What documentation do you provide for the adjuster's file?

Dated photographs, a written scope of damage, and moisture survey results where applicable, organized against the specific storm date being claimed.

Can you work with a public adjuster we've already hired?

Yes. We'll provide the same technical documentation to a public adjuster that we would to a carrier's adjuster.

How do you tell hail damage apart from a roof that was already near the end of its life?

We look at membrane condition, flashing wear, and drainage patterns across the entire roof rather than only the spot an owner points to, and we're direct about what we find either way.

items.name: TPO 60 Mil Roof Systems in Lansing, MI
items.meta: TPO 60 mil roof systems for Lansing, MI commercial buildings: freeze-thaw performance, cold-weather install windows, and ice-dam drainage details.

sections.html:
TPO 60 Mil: The Baseline Spec for Most Lansing Reroofs

Sixty mil TPO is the membrane thickness most Lansing building owners land on once they compare price against how long a roof actually has to survive freeze-thaw cycling, ice damming at parapets, and the wear that comes with rooftop HVAC service. It is not the cheapest option and it is not the toughest one. It is the middle spec that fits most office, retail, and institutional roofs across the region.

Thickness Is a Risk Decision, Not a Line Item

When we walk a roof with an owner, the first question is rarely what does 60 mil cost. It's what the roof has to tolerate over the next fifteen to twenty years: how many mechanical contractors will be on it every winter for furnace and RTU service, whether the parapets hold standing snow into March, and whether the building sits somewhere that gets skipped on plow routes and lets ice build against curbs. A 45 mil membrane can look identical to 60 mil on a bid sheet. It does not behave the same after five winters of contraction and expansion.

We frame the decision around what a shortfall costs later, not what the upgrade costs now. A membrane that's too thin for the traffic and weather load usually shows its first split or seam stress inside the warranty period, and by then the owner is paying for a service call, a patch, and the reputation damage of a leak over a tenant space or a server room.

What Freeze-Thaw Cycling Does to a Single-Ply Membrane

Michigan's swing between hard freeze and January thaw is harder on a roof than steady cold. TPO contracts as temperatures drop, and on a 60 mil sheet the seams and flashings absorb that movement better than a thinner membrane does. At parapet walls, curb flashings, and pipe penetrations, repeated contraction is where failures start. We look closely at those details on every 60 mil job because the field membrane rarely fails first, the terminations do.

Older buildings around Grand Ledge and Charlotte, where plant-era steel decking is common, add another variable. Deck fasteners and insulation boards installed decades ago don't always hold the way a modern deck does, and a freeze-thaw cycle exploits any give in the substrate before it shows up as a visible membrane problem.

Cold-Weather Installation Windows

Hot-air welding TPO seams requires the membrane and the ambient air to be within a workable temperature range, and that window closes fast once Lansing moves from a wet October into a hard November frost. We schedule 60 mil projects around that shift deliberately rather than pushing a crew to weld seams in marginal cold, because a rushed weld in cold weather is exactly the kind of failure point that doesn't show up until the following winter.

For owners planning a fall reroof, that means committing to an install date early enough to finish welding before temperatures drop consistently below the manufacturer's cold-weather threshold. If a project can't hit that window, we'd rather talk through a spring start than compromise seam quality to beat the calendar.

Ice Damming, Drainage, and Edge Detail

A 60 mil roof lives or dies at the edges. Scuppers, internal drains, and edge metal have to move water off the roof before it refreezes overnight and builds an ice dam against the membrane. We see this most on flat-roofed retail strips in Holt and South Lansing where drainage was designed for the original tenant footprint and never revisited after later renovations added rooftop units or reduced the number of working drains.

Buildings near the Meridian Mall corridor in Okemos and along Eastwood Towne Center in Lansing Township carry heavier snow loads because of taller adjacent structures that shade sections of the roof and slow melt. On those roofs we pay close attention to drain capacity and slope before we ever talk membrane thickness, because the best TPO in the world won't outlast standing meltwater against a parapet.

Where 60 Mil Fits the Building Stock We See

Government offices near the Capitol, school buildings, medical office buildings around Okemos, and mid-size retail across the region are where 60 mil consistently makes sense. The traffic load is moderate, the budget usually can't stretch to 80 mil across the whole roof, and the building doesn't have the kind of chemical or grease exposure that would push us toward PVC instead.

  • Membrane pulling away from fastening plates at seams
  • Visible shrinkage or tenting at parapet corners
  • Recurring leaks near the same roof drain after every thaw
  • Cracked or brittle pipe boots and curb flashings
  • Ponding water that no longer drains within 48 hours
  • Granule or coating loss exposing the reinforcement scrim

When we see two or more of those signs on a roof inspection, we tell the owner plainly that a patch is a short-term answer and a recover or replacement conversation needs to start before the next winter, not after a ceiling leak forces the issue.

What Owners Ask Before Approving a 60 Mil Reroof

Is 60 mil enough for a roof with regular HVAC service traffic?

For most office and retail roofs, yes, as long as walk pads or designated service paths protect the membrane at high-traffic zones near rooftop units. If a roof sees frequent equipment replacement or heavy foot traffic without protection, we'll talk about 80 mil for those roofs.

Can 60 mil TPO go over an existing EPDM roof?

Sometimes, if the existing roof passes a moisture scan and the deck is sound. We won't recommend a recover over wet insulation regardless of the new membrane's thickness, because trapped moisture will undermine the new system from underneath.

What happens if the weather turns before the install finishes?

We build schedule contingency into fall projects specifically because Michigan weather doesn't always cooperate. If a cold front arrives early, we'd rather pause and secure the roof properly than finish a weld outside the manufacturer's temperature spec.

Does 60 mil TPO handle ponding water?

It resists it better than modified bitumen, but membrane thickness doesn't fix a drainage problem. If a roof holds water past 48 hours, we look at tapered insulation or additional drains before we talk membrane spec.

How long should a properly installed 60 mil roof last in this climate?

With sound substrate, correct seam welding, and drainage that actually works, most 60 mil TPO roofs in this region perform well past the manufacturer's stated warranty term. The failures we get called for almost always trace back to a detail, not the field membrane itself.

items.name: TPO 80 Mil Roof Systems in Lansing, MI
items.meta: TPO 80 mil roofing for Lansing manufacturing, distribution, and mechanical-heavy commercial roofs: puncture resistance, snow load, and install timing.

sections.html:
When 80 Mil TPO Is Worth the Extra Cost in Lansing

Eighty mil TPO costs more per square than 60 mil, and on the right roof that difference pays for itself before the warranty is half over. We reach for 80 mil on roofs that carry heavy foot traffic, dense mechanical equipment, or the kind of punishing seasonal load that comes with a plant, a distribution building, or a roof serving heavy manufacturing tenants near the Delta Township and Lansing Grand River assembly corridors.

What Actually Justifies the Upgrade

We don't sell 80 mil as a default upgrade. Most retail and office roofs never need it. The membrane earns its cost on roofs with dense rooftop unit fields, frequent contractor traffic for equipment service, or exposure to falling debris and foot traffic from adjacent higher structures. Distribution and light-industrial buildings along the US-127 corridor through DeWitt Township and the I-96 industrial park near Fowlerville and Webberville are typical candidates.

The extra 20 mils of membrane thickness translates directly into puncture resistance. On a roof where technicians are climbing over conduit, walking tool bags across the field, or dragging ladders for lighting and HVAC service, that resistance is what keeps a small gouge from becoming a leak two winters later.

Freeze-Thaw Load on Mechanical-Heavy Roofs

Manufacturing and distribution roofs tend to carry more curbs, more penetrations, and more equipment than a typical office roof, and every one of those details is a place where freeze-thaw movement concentrates stress. An 80 mil membrane handles that repeated flexing at flashings better than a thinner sheet, which matters most on roofs that see dozens of service visits a year rather than two or three.

We've walked plant-era roofs around Grand Ledge and Charlotte where the original deck and curb work predates current fastening standards. On those roofs, the membrane thickness is only part of the equation; the condition of the curbs and the deck underneath determines whether an 80 mil install actually gets the service life it's priced for.

Cold-Weather Install Sequencing on Larger Roofs

Bigger roofs with more mechanical equipment take longer to install, which means the cold-weather welding window matters even more on an 80 mil job than a smaller retail reroof. We sequence these projects to front-load the field welding and push detail work at curbs and penetrations into the earliest part of the schedule, because those are the seams most likely to need rework if temperatures drop mid-project.

On a distribution building or plant roof, a delayed start can push seam welding into a window where the membrane doesn't reach the manufacturer's minimum application temperature reliably. We'd rather stage the project across two shorter fall windows than force a full install into marginal weather.

Snow Load and Edge Metal on Larger Spans

Wide-span industrial roofs collect more snow than a broken-up retail roofline, and that snow doesn't move evenly. Drifting against parapets and rooftop screens builds uneven load, and when it thaws and refreezes at the edge, it puts real pressure on edge metal and coping. We spec heavier gauge edge metal and check scupper sizing on these roofs specifically because an 80 mil membrane doesn't solve a drainage or edge-detail problem by itself.

  • Membrane punctures near HVAC curbs or conduit runs
  • Recurring foot-traffic wear paths worn through the surface
  • Edge metal separating from parapet coping after freeze-thaw cycles
  • Snow drift buildup against rooftop screens or equipment
  • Fastener backout visible under the membrane surface
  • Seam stress at high-density curb clusters

Most of those signs show up first at the details, not the open field, which is why our inspections spend more time at curbs, drains, and penetrations than walking the flat expanse of membrane.

Where We See 80 Mil Pay Off

Manufacturing support buildings, distribution centers, and plants with dense mechanical layouts near the automotive corridor are the clearest fit. We also spec it on schools and government buildings with rooftop mechanical rooms that see constant technician traffic, since the membrane cost difference is small relative to the building's overall roof budget.

We'll also spec it selectively on multi-tenant retail roofs where one tenant's kitchen or process equipment creates unusually heavy rooftop traffic compared to the rest of the building, even if the overall roof doesn't otherwise call for the upgrade.

Questions Owners Ask About 80 Mil TPO

Is 80 mil overkill for a small office building?

Usually. If the roof has light mechanical load and limited foot traffic, 60 mil performs just as well for less money. We'll say so directly rather than upselling a thickness the roof doesn't need.

Does thicker membrane mean a longer warranty automatically?

Not by itself. Warranty length depends on the manufacturer's program and the installed assembly, not membrane thickness alone. We walk owners through what's actually covered before they compare warranty terms across bids.

How does 80 mil perform under heavy snow load compared to 60 mil?

The membrane itself resists puncture and abrasion better, but snow load performance is mostly a structural and drainage question. We evaluate both together rather than treating membrane thickness as the whole answer.

Can we install 80 mil TPO over an existing modified bitumen roof?

Sometimes, if the existing roof is dry and structurally sound and code allows another layer. A moisture survey comes before that recommendation, not after.

What's the real cost difference between 60 and 80 mil on a large roof?

It varies by project, but the material cost gap is usually smaller than owners expect relative to total project cost, especially once labor, tear-off, and detail work are figured in. We'll show the actual number on the specific roof rather than a rule of thumb.
